posts.summary: Inspired by a coffee shop sign asking 'What would 2PAC do?', Frazelle reflects on Tupac's song 'Changes' and draws parallels to her own life decision to take a new job in New York. She connects Tupac's message about change with Gandhi's philosophy about self-transformation, concluding that while external inspiration is valuable, ultimately you must trust yourself to make important life decisions.
key_points: Change should be embraced when you're not happy with current circumstances
You have control over changing yourself even when you can't change everything
Career moves require leaving behind relationships that have become family
Trust yourself as your own best advocate when making decisions
money_quotes: If you are not happy with the way things are currently going, you should stand up and try to change it.
Some things will never change, but what you do have control of changing is yourself.
Over the past year, my co-workers have gone from being friends to being my family.
When it comes down to it you are your own best advocate.
Change can come about for a variety of reasons.
posts.key_insight: While external inspiration and role models can prompt reflection on major decisions, ultimately you must trust yourself as your own best advocate when choosing to embrace change.

posts.summary: This early blog post from Jess Frazelle documents her exploration of Foursquare's API to enable remote check-ins for badge collection. She reverse-engineered the check-in process using PHP and cURL, discovering the rate limits and geographic validation rules Foursquare implemented. The post serves as both a technical tutorial on OAuth authentication and API manipulation, and an early example of her characteristic approach to treating security boundaries as puzzles to solve.
key_points: Foursquare's API allows check-ins by passing venue coordinates, enabling location spoofing
Rate limiting kicks in after 15-20 rapid check-ins, requiring cooldown periods
Geographic validation requires waiting realistic travel time between distant locations
The post provides complete PHP code for OAuth flow and programmatic check-ins
money_quotes: I would just like to preface this by saying I do not condone cheating but I thought of this as a 'challenge' and not so much as 'cheating'.

But surely, surely foursquare must have some sort of catches in place that do not allow this. Because I was ever so curious to find out what they may be (...and how to get around them) I decided to try.
Ok I will admit it I am kinda a competitive person and well, the foursquare badges are so pretty I immediately started thinking about how I could check in remotely and collect them all.

When changing locations over a vast distance (ex. Los Angeles -> San Francisco), you must wait the amount of time it takes to reasonably cover that distance before checking in or else you will not be able to earn badges.
Haters gonna hate, but you just made foursquare yo biotch.
posts.key_insight: An early glimpse of Frazelle's hacker mindset: treating platform restrictions as technical puzzles to understand and circumvent through API exploration.

posts.summary: This tutorial walks through the complete process of installing Debian Linux on a MacBook Pro Retina, covering boot manager setup with rEFInd, disk partitioning, building a custom 3.17.x kernel for thunderbolt hotplugging support, and configuring drivers for wifi, graphics, and touchpad. Frazelle documents the numerous pain points and workarounds required, from dealing with EFI boot quirks to fixing CD-ROM mount errors during installation. The post reflects her frustration with macOS and her determination to run Linux as her primary OS, even sharing her custom kernel configs and shell scripts for backlight control.
key_points: rEFInd is the preferred boot manager over rEFIt for dual-booting
Kernel 3.17.x is required for thunderbolt hotplugging on Mac hardware
Building custom kernels is necessary for full hardware support
ext4 with overlay storage driver is recommended for Docker users
Most Mac hardware works on Linux except iSight camera and screen brightness
money_quotes: I really hope you didn't just copy, paste, and run a command on your host without knowing anything about the author.
I was so fed up with OSX and the fact I never used it, I nuked it entirely.
Obviously my favorite is Debian Jessie, so I will go into detail how to make a USB boot drive for that, but you can substitute out whatever sub-par distro you choose.
Do you understand now why rEFInd is the hardest part? It's like iptables, change one thing and everything comes crashing down.
Login as root, yes I know you just created an actual user in the installation steps but ROOT ACCESS OR DEATH.
The retina resolution is absolutely stunning, it's seriously hard for me to switch to my Thinkpad which has 32GB of memory (so I should want to switch).
posts.key_insight: Running Linux on Mac hardware requires significant effort including custom kernel builds and driver configuration, but the result—especially thunderbolt hotplugging and retina display support—makes it worthwhile for those committed to escaping macOS.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle demonstrates an unconventional use of Docker: running desktop applications in containers on Linux. Motivated by the desire for OS X-style application sandboxing and clean uninstalls, she shows how to containerize everything from text-based apps (IRC, email, Twitter clients) to full GUI applications (Chrome, Spotify, Skype, Tor Browser) by mounting the X11 socket. This approach provides resource control (CPU/memory limits), application isolation, and the satisfaction of knowing deleted apps leave no traces behind.
key_points: Docker can run GUI applications by mounting the X11 socket rather than using SSH X11 forwarding
Containerizing desktop apps provides resource control - you can limit CPU and memory usage
This approach ensures clean uninstalls with no leftover files scattered across the system
The technique works for everything from simple TUI apps to complex GUI apps requiring sound and video
money_quotes: I use Docker to run all the desktop apps on my computers.
I hate installing things on my host and the files getting everywhere. I wanted the ability to delete an app and know it is gone fully without some random file hanging around.
Yes, the cpu/memory hungry chrome is now perfectly contained!
Because why should you ever have to install ssh into a container? EWWW UNNECESSARY BLOAT!
The images work by mounting the X11 socket into the container! Yippeeeee!
posts.key_insight: Docker containers aren't just for production deployments—they can sandbox desktop applications, giving you resource control and clean isolation that traditional package managers can't provide.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle explains how she manages Docker's testing infrastructure of ~50 servers as a part-time responsibility while working as a core maintainer. The infrastructure tests Docker across 6 storage drivers, multiple Linux distros, various kernel versions, and Windows clients, processing hundreds of builds weekly. By running everything in Docker containers—Jenkins, Consul, IRC bots, webhook services—and using tools like Consul for monitoring, the system essentially runs itself, allowing her to focus on her primary engineering work.
key_points: Docker's tests run in Docker containers across 6 storage drivers (aufs, btrfs, devmapper, overlay, vfs, zfs)
Every push to master triggers builds on 8+ servers; every PR triggers 3 builds on different servers
Consul running in containers on all 50 servers enables tracking kernel versions, storage drivers, and distros to identify bugs
Custom tooling (leeroy, gordonbot, docker-bb, nsqexec) all runs containerized to manage builds and deployments
Dogfooding Docker RC builds and master binaries catches bugs before releases
money_quotes: This blog post is going to be about how I manage ~50 servers but don't do anything at all.
I would honestly be lost without consul.
We were able to fix kernel bugs that were specific to those versions related to networking before an RC was even cut.
The greatest thing about all these services, which I so subtly mentioned, running in containers is that we can always be dogfooding and testing Docker.

All this is so seamless and runs so well that I have time to do my actual job of being a Docker core maintainer, occasionally fix some servers, spin up new servers if we add storage drivers, upgrade servers' kernels, and write this blog post.
posts.key_insight: Running your entire testing infrastructure in the same technology you're building creates a virtuous cycle where dogfooding catches bugs early while automation keeps operational overhead minimal.

posts.summary: This technical tutorial demonstrates how to route all host traffic through a Tor Docker container using iptables rules. Frazelle provides a straightforward approach: run her Tor image with host networking, then apply iptables NAT and filter rules to redirect all TCP traffic through Tor's transparent proxy port while allowing local network exceptions. The post acknowledges the tradeoff of using --net host but argues it's pragmatic given the goal of routing all traffic.
key_points: Run Tor in Docker with --net host to simplify traffic routing
Use iptables NAT rules to redirect DNS and TCP traffic through Tor's TransPort
Allow exceptions for local network ranges that shouldn't route through Tor
Filter rules ensure only Tor's UID can make direct outbound connections
money_quotes: It's actually a lot simplier than you would think. But it involves dealing with some unsavory things such as iptables.

Easy right? I can already hear the haters, 'blah blah blah net host'. Chill out, the point is to route all our traffic duhhhh so we may as well, otherwise would need to change / overwrite some of Docker's iptables rules, and really who has time for that shit…
You do? Ok make a PR to this blog post.
Contain yourselves, I am about to throw down some sick iptables rules.
posts.key_insight: Containerizing Tor and using host networking with iptables provides a clean, reproducible way to route all system traffic through the Tor network without complex Docker networking workarounds.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle demonstrates how to build a custom Docker container for R-based data science work, driven by her philosophy of never installing tools directly on the host machine. She walks through creating a Dockerfile with popular R packages like ggplot2 and the Wes Anderson color palette, then shows how to run it with X11 forwarding for graphical output. The post is a quick, practical tutorial written during what was supposed to be her vacation.
key_points: Docker containers provide a clean way to run R data science environments without polluting the host system
X11 forwarding enables graphical output from containerized R sessions
Custom Dockerfiles let data scientists bundle their preferred R packages into reproducible environments
Bind mounts allow containers to access local data files for analysis
money_quotes: You probably know by now I hate installing things on my host.
Can you imagine if the percentage of packages contributed by Hadley to CRAN was mirrored by someone to NPM or pip? It would be crazy.
So it turns out I'm pretty bad at vacation. I had this idea for a blog post and one thing lead to another and here we are…
See that was super easy, now I can go back to being on vacation and reading the latest Vogue.
posts.key_insight: Running R data science workflows inside Docker containers keeps the host clean and makes analytical environments reproducible and portable.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle confronts the severe harassment she has faced as a woman in the tech industry, describing threats of sexual violence, death threats, and graphic disturbing images sent to her since she began speaking at conferences and contributing to open source. She expresses frustration that she cannot simply do the work she loves without enduring this abuse. Despite the hostility, she refuses to leave the industry or change who she is.
key_points: Women in open source and tech conferences face extreme, ongoing harassment
The harassment ranges from sexual messages to death threats to graphic violent imagery
The abuse is a direct consequence of being visible in the industry
She refuses to be driven out despite the hostile environment
money_quotes: My least favorite topic in the world is 'Women in Tech', so I am going to make this short but I think it's something that needs to be said.
Ever since I started speaking at conferences and contributing to open source projects I have been endlessly harassed.
I've gotten hundreds of private messages on IRC and emails about sex, rape, and death threats.
I wish I could do my job, something I very obviously love doing, without any of this bullshit.
But I'm not leaving and I'm not going to stop being me. So this is me saying 'Fuck You.'
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's harassment problem is so severe that women cannot participate visibly in open source and conferences without enduring threats of violence, yet refusing to be silenced is itself an act of resistance.

posts.summary: This tutorial walks through how to set up three types of Tor relays (bridge, middle, and exit) using Docker containers built on Alpine Linux. The post explains the differences between relay types, with bridge relays being unlisted for censorship resistance, middle relays forwarding traffic, and exit relays being the publicly visible endpoint. It provides complete Dockerfiles and torrc configurations for each type, including a reduced exit policy that limits which ports accept traffic to minimize abuse complaints.
key_points: Three types of Tor relays serve different roles: bridge (unlisted, helps bypass censorship), middle (forwards traffic), and exit (publicly visible endpoint that receives takedown notices)
Docker and Alpine Linux make running a Tor relay lightweight — the entire image is only 11.52MB
Exit relays should use a reduced exit policy to limit which ports route traffic, minimizing potential abuse and takedown notices
Running a relay is a way to give back to the Tor community — if you use Tor, you should consider hosting one
money_quotes: I figured it was important, if you are going to be a tor user, to document how you can help the Tor community by hosting a Tor relay.
Exit relays tend to be the ones recieving take down notices because the IP is the one the public sees traffic from Tor as.
If you don't want to host an exit node, host a middle relay instead! And if you want your relay not publically listed in the network then host a bridge.
The more people who run relays, the faster the Tor network will be.
posts.key_insight: Docker's lightweight containerization makes it trivially easy to contribute to the Tor network by running a relay, lowering the barrier for privacy infrastructure participation.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle demonstrates how to set up a Tor SOCKS5 proxy and a Privoxy HTTP proxy as separate Docker containers, continuing her series on combining Docker with Tor. She walks through the Dockerfiles, configuration changes, and runtime commands for both containers, showing how to link them together. The approach emphasizes container best practices by keeping each service in its own container rather than bundling multiple processes together.
key_points: A Tor SOCKS5 proxy can be containerized in an Alpine-based Docker image weighing only 11.51 MB
Privoxy can be linked to the Tor proxy container to provide an HTTP proxy interface to the Tor network
Separating services into individual containers eliminates the need for init systems managing multiple processes
The SocksPort must bind to 0.0.0.0 to work correctly with the container's network namespace
money_quotes: I prefer my approach of 2 different containers, because it is cleaner, maybe sometimes you do not need both, and you completely eliminate the need for having an init system starting 2 processes in one container.
This image weighs in at only 11.51 MB!
Now you can import and search for keys on a key server with improved anonymity.
You could even run chrome in a container through the proxy…
posts.key_insight: Running Tor and Privoxy as separate linked Docker containers provides flexible, lightweight privacy proxies while adhering to the container principle of one process per container.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle connects a talk on decompression bombs to Docker's cgroup-based I/O throttling capabilities. After seeing a presentation on malicious compressed files that expand to consume massive resources, she highlights Docker's blkio read/write bandwidth controls as a practical defense mechanism. She demonstrates how the --read-bps-device flag can limit container I/O throughput using cgroups, effectively throttling resource consumption at the device level.
key_points: Decompression bombs are malicious compressed files designed to consume excessive resources when extracted
Docker's blkio cgroup support allows throttling read/write bandwidth per device
The --read-bps-device flag controls the upper limit on I/O to a device but does not control disk space utilization
Cgroups provide a practical containment mechanism against resource exhaustion attacks
money_quotes: Most programs will not decompress the files recursively. Which just leaves us with the problem of the more sofisticated decompression bomb.
Granted, this does not control disk space utilization, BUT it does allow for throttling the upper limit on write/read to the device.
posts.key_insight: Docker's cgroup-based I/O throttling provides a practical defense layer against resource exhaustion attacks like decompression bombs by limiting bandwidth at the device level.

posts.summary: The post explains how to set up a reverse VPN to securely expose services from a home network to the public internet, rather than the traditional use of VPNs for accessing office networks remotely. Jess walks through the complete Docker-based setup using OpenVPN, from generating certificates on a cloud instance to configuring the client on a home NAS. The key insight is using Docker's network namespace sharing to route specific containers through the VPN tunnel, giving fine-grained control over what gets exposed.
key_points: Reverse VPNs flip the traditional model - instead of accessing private networks from outside, they expose home services to the public
Using Docker containers for both the VPN server and client provides clean isolation and easy deployment
The --net container:openvpn flag lets you selectively route only specific containers through the VPN tunnel
This setup is useful for home automation webhooks, media servers, and other services that need public accessibility
money_quotes: A reverse VPN is for exposing things from your home network into the public. Why? Well for one, you shouldn't want to expose your home network to the world. There are a lot of risks in doing that. A reverse VPN allows you to securely control what you are exposing.
Of course on my NAS, I have docker installed, but you can install openvpn on your host too (but I will judge you).
Now all you have to do it run all your other containers in --net container:openvpn!
Alternatively if you would rather run everything on your server over the vpn you can run the openvpn container with --net host. I personally like the control though.
posts.key_insight: Docker's network namespace sharing combined with a reverse VPN creates a secure, granular way to expose specific home services to the internet without opening up your entire network.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle explains how to create custom seccomp profiles for Docker containers, walking through her experience generating a profile for Chrome by running it under strace for an entire day. She demonstrates that even with automated tools, the resulting profile missed 6 critical syscalls that required manual debugging and domain knowledge to identify. The post serves as both a tutorial and an argument for why Docker needed to ship sane default seccomp profiles that protect users without requiring this painful manual process.
key_points: Docker merged a default seccomp profile that blocks dangerous syscalls while maintaining compatibility
Creating custom seccomp profiles requires running containers under strace and collecting syscall data
Automated profile generation tools miss syscalls - her script missed 6 including unshare, setns, setgid, and exit_group
Debugging missing syscalls requires domain knowledge about how applications use system calls
The default profile exists so users don't have to go through hours of painful debugging
money_quotes: So this is not the most sane thing in the world, I even tried in the process to create a bash script that takes the output from strace, collects the syscalls, and generates a profile. But like all tools of this sort it missed some, well to be exact it missed 6.
So I used chrome the entire day like this to create the most verbose strace output so I wouldn't miss any syscalls.
Now you have a whitelist generated from your strace output. But it's super bad and when you try to run your container with it you get a vague error and Operation not permitted.
So now we have to use our brains. WHAT!? NOOOOO!

Obviously noone else is going to do this, debug for hours the syscalls that are missing. This is why the default profile is so important, we wanted to create sane defaults that would protect people but also not cause all this pain.
We are working on sane security profiles for containers that don't make you want to pull your hair out.
posts.key_insight: Good security defaults matter because the manual alternative of creating custom seccomp profiles requires deep systems knowledge and hours of painful debugging that no normal user will actually do.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle demonstrates how to run desktop applications and Docker-in-Docker using Docker's new user namespace support coming in version 1.10. She walks through practical examples including Chrome, the notify-osd daemon with irssi IRC client integration, and explains how to achieve Docker-in-Docker without the --privileged flag by carefully selecting only the necessary capabilities (CAP_SYS_ADMIN and CAP_NET_ADMIN) and adjusting seccomp/apparmor profiles.
key_points: User namespace support moved from Docker Experimental to stable in version 1.10.0
Running Chrome in a container with user namespaces only requires adding --group-add video and --group-add audio
Desktop notification daemons can be containerized and shared with other containers via volume sharing
Docker-in-Docker without --privileged is possible by using only CAP_SYS_ADMIN and CAP_NET_ADMIN capabilities
The --privileged flag can be replaced with more granular security controls
money_quotes: Now I have always run my notifications daemon in a container, because that stuff is nasty to install, so many dependencies, ewwww.
What is --privileged actually doing? Well for starters it's allowing all capabilites, but… do we really need them all? The answer is no.. all we really need is CAP_SYS_ADMIN and CAP_NET_ADMIN.
Makes sense right.. we obviously want to be a member of those groups to watch Taylor Swift music videos.

When running the docker daemon with user namespace support, you cannot use docker run flags like --privileged, --net host, --pid host, etc. These are for pretty obvious reasons so I'm not going to get into it, if you want to know more RTFM.
posts.key_insight: The --privileged flag is a sledgehammer when you usually only need a scalpel—understanding which specific Linux capabilities you actually need enables more secure container configurations.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle documents her migration from Docker to runc for running desktop applications in containers, motivated by the desire for fine-grained control over user namespace mappings. She needed custom gid mappings to access audio and video devices while still benefiting from user namespace isolation. To facilitate this workflow, she built three tools: Riddler (converts Docker containers to OCI specs), Netns (provides bridge networking for runc containers), and Magneto (displays container stats).
key_points: Migrated all desktop containers from Docker to runc for more control over user namespace mappings
Created Riddler to convert Docker container inspect output to OCI spec format
Built Netns to provide simple bridge networking for runc containers via prestart hooks
Developed Magneto as a stats viewer for runc containers, piping from runc events
money_quotes: This is the awesome thing about runc. You, the user, have all the control.
Then you can do cool things like listen to Taylor Swift in a container with a user namespace.
In layman's terms, I basically wanted to poke a teeny tiny map in the user namespace to be able to have permission to use my sound and video devices.
Keep in mind since these are generated a lot of the filepaths are hardcoded for things on my host. So if you try to run these and aren't me I don't want to hear any whining.
posts.key_insight: When higher-level container tools don't expose the low-level controls you need, dropping down to runc gives you complete power over namespace mappings and container configuration.

posts.summary: Docker 1.10 introduced the ability to assign specific IP addresses to containers through libnetwork, eliminating the need for hacky workarounds like pipework or custom bash scripts. This feature allows users with dedicated IP blocks to easily assign public IPs directly to containers with simple commands. Jess demonstrates the straightforward three-step process: create a network with your subnet, run a container with a specific IP, and access it publicly.
key_points: Docker 1.10 added native support for specifying container IP addresses
Previously required workarounds like pipework or custom scripts
Simple workflow: create network with subnet, run container with --ip flag
Enables direct public IP assignment to containers
money_quotes: This is so cool I can hardly stand it.
I totally use these for good and not for evil.
But to use these previously with Docker containers meant hackery with the awesome pipework. Or even worse some homegrown, Jess bash scripts.
But now MY LIFE JUST GOT SO MUCH EASIER.
posts.key_insight: Native IP assignment in Docker 1.10 transforms a previously hacky container networking task into a simple three-command workflow.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle provides an unfiltered evaluation of Docker's storage drivers (graphdrivers), examining the tradeoffs of each option. She advocates for Overlay as her personal choice despite some kernel bugs, dismisses Devicemapper as unreliable outside RedHat, and warns that each driver has significant setup requirements or limitations. The post cuts through marketing to give practitioners real-world guidance on which storage backend to choose.
key_points: Overlay requires kernel 3.18+ and backported versions ('frankenkernels') shouldn't be trusted
Aufs works well but isn't in the kernel by default, requiring extra packages
Btrfs and ZFS both require disk partitioning or kernel module setup that creates adoption friction
Devicemapper only works properly on RedHat and requires perfect configuration
VFS should only be used for testing
money_quotes: Frankenkernels are not to be trusted. This is not to say it won't work, hey it might work great, but it's not to be trusted.
Aufs is another great one. But it is not in the kernel by default which blows.

My mom once asked her friend for her famous chicken enchilada recipe so she could make it herself. The friend gave the recipe but left out one key ingredient so that my mom's never tasted just right. There was always something off about it. This is how I think of devicemapper.
It works on RedHat.
I sure hope to hell you are just testing something or clinically insane.
posts.key_insight: Every Docker storage driver has significant tradeoffs, but Overlay with a modern kernel is the most practical choice for most users, while Devicemapper should be avoided unless you're on RedHat.

posts.summary: The post argues that while containers and sandboxes use the same Linux primitives (namespaces, seccomp, AppArmor), containers aren't true sandboxes primarily because they require root privileges to create and run. By leveraging user namespaces, it's possible to create containers as an unprivileged user, though cgroups remain a challenge. The author presents binctr as a proof of concept and advocates for evolving containers toward real sandbox isolation.
key_points: Containers and sandboxes share the same Linux primitives but differ in privilege requirements
Cgroups control what a process can use, namespaces control what it can see
User namespaces enable unprivileged container creation with specific uid/gid mapping constraints
Generic containers are too universal to provide true sandbox-level security
The scientific community has strong interest in unprivileged containers due to restrictions on running as root
money_quotes: One of the key differences between how you run Chrome and how you run a container are the privileges used. Chrome runs as your own unprivileged user. Most containers (be it docker, runc, or rkt) run as root.
Cgroups control what a process can use. Whereas namespaces control what a process can see.
A generic container will never be equivalent to a sandbox because it's too universal to really lock down the application.
Containers are not going to be the answer to preventing your application from being compromised, but they can limit the damage from a compromise.
If we ignore, for the time being, this huge tire fire that is creating cgroups as an unprivileged user, then unprivileged containers are easy.
posts.key_insight: True container sandboxing requires running without root privileges, which user namespaces can enable, but the real security value comes from application-specific profiles rather than generic container configurations.

posts.summary: Being an open source maintainer requires learning to say 'no' to patches, which is one of the hardest parts of the job. The post outlines several techniques for closing pull requests gracefully while minimizing contributor frustration, including the 'ego stroke and close,' closing early before contributors invest more work, and 'carrying' patches to completion when contributors disappear. Frazelle emphasizes that maintainers deal with an information asymmetry where contributors treat their PRs as pets while maintainers must manage them as cattle.
key_points: Saying 'no' to patches is one of the hardest parts of being an open source maintainer
Close early if you know a patch won't be accepted - don't make contributors do unnecessary work
Use the 'ego stroke and close' technique to soften rejections with genuine appreciation
When carrying a patch, keep the original contributor's commits and don't close the original PR until the new one is opened
Information asymmetry exists: contributors invest emotionally in individual PRs while maintainers manage many
money_quotes: Rule #1 of open-source: no is temporary, yes is forever.
One of the numerous examples of information asymmetry in open source: contributors put effort in a pet PR, but maintainers manage cattle.
No one wants to have to do 300 rebases before learning the design of their patch isn't approved.
If you close it before opening the new one, the contributor will assume you are lying and never going to do it.
None of the suggestions below are going to save you from that person hate mailing you since you didn't merge their patch. But hey anything helps.
posts.key_insight: Closing pull requests is an unavoidable skill for maintainers, and doing it with empathy and clear communication—while being decisive and timely—minimizes frustration for everyone involved.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle presents a satirical 'BuzzFeed-style' listicle of her favorite linker flags, combining genuine technical utility with irreverent humor. The post covers flags for static linking, symbol management, debugging, and stripping binaries. While playfully mocking clickbait formats, it actually provides useful reference material for developers working with the linker.
key_points: Static linking (-static) is presented as the most important flag for creating self-contained binaries
Flags like --export-dynamic and --whole-archive enable advanced techniques like dlopen'ing yourself
Symbol stripping flags help reduce binary size for constrained environments
The -nostdlib flag provides escape from corrupted library paths
money_quotes: I would be an embarassment to myself if I didn't start with the flag that tells the linker to not link against shared libraries. This is the best flag. STATIC BINARIES FTW.
But with this flag, you tell it 'YOLO, I want it all' so that later you can dlopen yourself with that symbol that was never actually used until runtime. FUN!
If say you are a few KB/MB off from your binary fitting on a floppy disk, this flag is your friend.
This is nice when *someone* completely messes with your library path and the world is burning and you just want to link to those things you put in some random directory somewhere.
This flag is helpful when telling the linker you DGAF about unresolved symbols and to stop yelling at you.
posts.key_insight: Static binaries and understanding linker flags give developers precise control over their build artifacts, enabling everything from minimal deployments to advanced runtime tricks like self-dlopen.

posts.summary: Frazelle shares a childhood memory from 1995 Phoenix when varnish rags left by painters spontaneously combusted in the extreme Arizona heat, causing a house fire that destroyed much of her family's home. The post reflects on how losing material possessions—including her beloved teddy bears—taught her an early lesson about what truly matters. She closes with an eerie coincidence: a fire-scorched page of the Ten Commandments from discarded Sunday school books landed at their front door and remains framed in her parents' home today.
key_points: Varnish rags combined with 120°F Arizona heat caused spontaneous combustion
The fire spread from trash cans to her parents' bedroom
No one was home during the fire—family members were scattered elsewhere
Her father discovered the fire by following fire trucks heading toward their home
The experience taught her that relationships matter more than material objects
money_quotes: Varnish rags are flammable and in combination with the Arizona heat this caused spontaneous combustion. I kid you not.
The only thing I could think about was how I left my favorite teddy bears with an 'I owe you' note that they could come to the next sleepover. Which of course would never happen.
This story is interesting for, of course, the moral lesson I learned at a young age that material objects don't matter. It's the relationships with people that do.
I'm not a religious person, on my Facebook page my religion is literally 'hugs' so take this as you want.
posts.key_insight: A childhood house fire taught the valuable lesson that material possessions are ultimately replaceable, but relationships with people are what truly matter.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle uses BigQuery to analyze GitHub Archive data, examining pull request statistics across popular repositories in 2015. She calculates merge ratios for top projects by unique contributors, finding that Docker had a 75.79% merge rate. The analysis culminates in comparing the average size of merged vs. closed pull requests to test the hypothesis that smaller PRs are more likely to be merged.
key_points: Top repositories by PR count include openmicroscopy/snoopys-sandbox, liferay-portal, and Homebrew
Many popular repos (like Apache Spark and Homebrew) show 0 merged PRs because they don't use GitHub's merge functionality
Docker/docker had 5,250 closed PRs with 882 unique authors and a 75.79% merge ratio
Merged PRs consistently have smaller diffs than closed PRs across most repositories
For Docker specifically, merged PRs averaged 176 additions vs 334 for closed PRs
money_quotes: Which got me wondering about my favorite subject ever, The Art of Closing.
Oh that is super weird. After looking into a few of the repos with 0 merged, it seems they aren't really using GitHub for merges.
We *know* that it is always better to make a small pull request to have it merged. Let's see if we can prove that with data!
IT IS PROVEN!!!
posts.key_insight: Data from GitHub's top repositories confirms the conventional wisdom that smaller pull requests are significantly more likely to be merged than larger ones.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle reflects on the growing appeal of leaving the tech industry, noting that at only 27 years old, departure already feels imminent. She describes fantasies of teaching math abroad, writing, or playing professional poker after leaving. Despite her frustrations, she's not ready to quit yet and remains committed to helping more women feel welcome in tech, even as she acknowledges having a 'hate/love' relationship with the industry.
key_points: At 27, leaving tech already feels imminent
Leaving would mean completely stopping open source contributions
Current motivation is helping more women feel welcome in tech
Maintains hope that future generations won't have to fight as hard to be heard
money_quotes: It seems imminent. I'm only 27 and let me repeat: it seems imminent.
The day I leave the tech industry will feel like a giant weight has finally been lifted. It will be freeing.
The day I leave the tech industry will be the day I contribute my last piece of code to open source software.
Today, I am not quite ready to give up this thing I have such a 'hate/love' relationship with.
Maybe we won't have to fight so hard just to be heard; to have our opinions matter.
I am not just 'the container girl'. I am a human being with feelings, a limit, and a future outside of tech.
posts.key_insight: Even highly accomplished engineers can feel exhausted by the tech industry's environment, viewing departure as an eventual relief rather than a loss.

posts.summary: Frazelle examines the tension between personal passion for open source projects and corporate interests when getting paid to maintain them. Drawing from her experience on Docker's core team, she argues that maintainers can and should stand up for project integrity while keeping their jobs. The post outlines practical lessons for companies backing open source projects, emphasizing that community trust depends on everyone playing by the same rules regardless of employment status.
key_points: Passion drives open source contribution - people who believe in and use a project contribute most heavily
Companies should hire maintainers from existing community members who already have passion for the project
Maintainership must be earned through contributions, not granted automatically with employment
Core teams must be empowered to say NO to substandard patches, even from internal company teams
Code review approvals (LGTMs) are permanent and tied to individuals, not corporations
Explicit contribution guidelines ensure everyone plays by the same rules, building community trust
money_quotes: Getting paid to work on open source is a rare and wonderful opportunity, but you should not have to give up your passion in the process. Your passion should be why companies want to pay you.
When an employee joins your company they should not automatically get push access to the project. Everyone must play by the same rules. EVERYONE.
You cannot get a LGTM from a corporation; you get it from an individual. I have never seen a company with a GitHub account going around doing code reviews. People do code reviews.
You may get paid by the company but you will protect the project at all costs, because at the end of the day, the project and the community were what you were a part of first.
When everyone plays by the same rules, your community will trust you the most.
posts.key_insight: Corporate-backed open source succeeds when companies recognize that maintainers' passion and loyalty to the project must come before company interests, and when everyone—employees and external contributors alike—plays by the same rules.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle documents the chaos that ensued when she changed her GitHub username from 'jfrazelle' to 'jessfraz' to match her other online handles. What started as a simple username swap cascaded into broken Jenkins builds, failed Go imports across 50+ repositories, and potential breakage for other developers who depended on her repos. She walks through her systematic approach to fixing everything using sed, vim's argdo command, and pull requests, while maintaining her characteristic humor about self-inflicted infrastructure pain.
key_points: Changing a GitHub username breaks Go imports that reference the old username path
Jenkins DSL configurations need manual intervention when the DSL repo itself can't be cloned
The vim 'argdo' command can batch-apply changes across multiple open buffers efficiently
Squatting on old usernames prevents malicious actors from creating fake repos to trick Go imports
Self-hosted infrastructure (private Jenkins, private Docker registry) gives more control but requires more maintenance
money_quotes: One thing you must know about me is that at no point was I thinking I hate this. I actually love stuff like this, I live for pain. Why else would I run Linux on the desktop?
Vendor your crap kids!!!
I'm going to need some more tires for this fire.
I am for sure going to hell for this. What have I done?
Also I have a deep fear of someone taking my old username and making fake repos to try and trick imports in Go so I figured I will squat on it forever to avoid this.
posts.key_insight: Infrastructure changes that seem simple can cascade into unexpected breakage across interconnected systems, but systematic scripting and a tolerance for self-inflicted pain can get you through.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle describes her quest to build the ultimate Linux desktop experience by modifying CoreOS Container Linux (a server OS) to run on her laptop with graphics drivers, then running all applications in rootless containers. She hacked Gentoo-based ebuilds to add Intel graphics support, set up auto-updates via an Omaha server, and leveraged the read-only /usr filesystem to enforce containerization of everything. The setup represents her vision of a secure, automatically-updating desktop where even the container runtime doesn't require root privileges.
key_points: Modified CoreOS Container Linux (a server OS) to work as a desktop OS by adding graphics drivers
Uses rootless containers exclusively - the container spawner itself doesn't run as root
Leverages Container Linux's auto-update system (based on Google's Omaha) for seamless OS updates
The read-only /usr filesystem forces everything to run in containers
Plans to migrate from X11 to Wayland (via sway) for better security
money_quotes: So just remember that before you get your panties in a knot on HackerNews because you live and die by Xmonad (I live and die by i3, fight me).
Yes I know I am insane to go through this much effort for my own laptop but whatever.

The filesystem is setup perfectly for running containers, there is a read-only /usr and a stateful read/write /. The data stored on / will never be manipulated by the update process. Plus since /usr is read-only it really forces you to run everything in containers.

You see the problem with both Docker and Runc as they are today is that they must be run as root. And I'm not talking about the process in the container. I'm talking about the container spawner itself.
So overall everything runs in containers, I can automatically update my operating system, and the containers are NOT running as root on my host. This is the dream and reality.
posts.key_insight: A truly secure containerized desktop requires not just running apps in containers, but ensuring the container runtime itself doesn't need root privileges, combined with an immutable base OS that enforces containerization by design.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle argues that containers are fundamentally different from Solaris Zones, BSD Jails, and VMs because containers are not first-class objects—they're built from Linux primitives (namespaces and cgroups) rather than being designed as top-level isolation mechanisms. This architectural difference gives containers unique flexibility, like sharing namespaces between processes, but also introduces complexity that leads to security bugs. She advocates for improving container security through hardening rather than abandoning them for alternatives that serve different use cases.
key_points: Containers are not real things—they're combinations of Linux namespaces and cgroups, not first-class primitives
Zones, Jails, and VMs are pre-designed isolation units; containers are building blocks you assemble
Namespace sharing enables unique debugging and tooling patterns impossible with other isolation methods
The flexibility of containers comes at the cost of complexity, which leads to security vulnerabilities
Container security can be improved through Seccomp, AppArmor, and SELinux profiles
money_quotes: A 'container' is just a term people use to describe a combination of Linux namespaces and cgroups. Linux namespaces and cgroups ARE first class objects. NOT containers.
VMs, Jails, and Zones are if you bought the legos already put together AND glued. So it's basically the Death Star and you don't have to do any work you get it pre-assembled out of the box.
Containers come with just the pieces so while the box says to build the Death Star, you are not tied to that. You can build two boats connected by a flipping ocean and no one is going to stop you.
Less is more, and the less complexity you have the less likely you will have odd, edge case bugs.

The confusion with containers primarily lies in assuming they fulfill the same use case as the others; which they do not. Containers allow for a flexibility and control that is not possible with Jails, Zones, or VMs. And THAT IS A FEATURE.
posts.key_insight: Containers' flexibility and security trade-offs stem from being assembled from primitives rather than designed as first-class isolation units, making them fundamentally different tools than VMs, Zones, or Jails—not inferior ones.

posts.summary: This post explains two Linux kernel objects that aren't namespaced in containers: time and the kernel keyring. Frazelle demonstrates that changing time inside a container affects the host system, and that keys stored in the kernel keyring by one container are visible to all other containers on the same host. She emphasizes that Docker's default seccomp profiles block these potentially dangerous operations, reinforcing that namespaces and cgroups alone don't provide complete container isolation.
key_points: Time is not namespaced - changing time in a container changes it on the host
The kernel keyring is shared across all containers on a host
Docker's default seccomp profiles block dangerous syscalls like settimeofday
CAP_SYS_TIME capability is required to change time and is not granted by default
A time namespace could be useful for testing scenarios but would create NTP complexity
Container isolation requires more than just namespaces and cgroups
money_quotes: Linux namespaces are one of the primitives that make up what is known as a 'container.' They control what a process can see. Cgroups, the other main ingredient of 'containers', control what a process can use.
Well, it's not namespaced so the time on the host would change as well. 'But whaaaaa? I thought containers were just like a VM', you ask.
Well if root in one container stores keys in the keyring, any other containers on that same host can see it in their keyring, which is really just the same exact keyring.
All in all, I hope this proves once again that you need more than just namespaces and cgroups to get any sort of 'real' isolation with containers.
Please, please don't disable seccomp or add extra capabilities you don't need.
posts.key_insight: Containers provide incomplete isolation by default because critical kernel objects like time and the keyring aren't namespaced, making seccomp profiles and capability restrictions essential for security.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle argues that security tools have historically been too difficult to use, causing users to disable them entirely. She advocates for finding the right balance between security and usability through collaboration between security and software engineers. Using Docker's default seccomp and AppArmor profiles as examples of security made invisible to users, she proposes a future where programming languages generate seccomp filters at build-time, allowing binaries to sandbox themselves automatically.
key_points: Security tooling traditionally requires users to jump through hoops or learn complex interfaces, leading to poor adoption
Docker's default AppArmor and seccomp profiles demonstrate how security can be enabled by default without users realizing it
Runtime-generated security profiles consistently fail because something is always missed during profiling
Build-time generated seccomp filters could ensure ALL code paths are covered, though challenges remain with exec'd binaries and plugins
Collaboration between security engineers and software engineers is essential for truly usable security
money_quotes: There is a point at which better security comes at the expense of convenience. This needs to stop happening. Stop compromising convenience for security.

When you combine expertise in different areas you build better products. This is not rocket science. However egos tend to get in the way as well as biases towards people who know and like the same things you do.
By making a default for all containers, we can secure a very large amount of users without them even realizing it's happening.
Generating security filters/profiles at runtime has been done in the past & failed… over and over and over again. Something is always missed while profiling the application.
When the 'thing that was missed' is called and blocked, users will just turn off the 'security.' This happens all the time with things like SELinux and AppArmor.
Hopefully my dream of making binaries sandbox themselves will eventually get there. I know I won't stop until it does.
posts.key_insight: The best security is invisible security—enabled by default, requiring no user configuration, and generated deterministically at build-time rather than through error-prone runtime profiling.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle explores Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) after joining Microsoft, explaining how it works at a technical level including Pico processes, system call translation, and the ability to run Linux binaries natively without a VM. She walks through the architecture where WSL kernel drivers translate Linux syscalls to Windows NT APIs, discusses practical features like launching Windows executables from WSL and running X applications, and shares pain points around copy/paste, file performance, and filesystem interoperability. The post also covers how to set up a Windows machine reproducibly using Boxstarter.
key_points: WSL uses Pico processes and kernel drivers (lxcore.sys, lxss.sys) to translate Linux syscalls to Windows NT APIs
The Windows NT kernel was designed from the beginning to support POSIX and already had fork implemented
WSL allows launching Windows GUI apps from Linux and running X applications with an X server
File operations are slow in WSL because Windows isn't optimized for many small files
Boxstarter enables reproducible Windows machine setup similar to Linux scripting
money_quotes: I am super excited about Windows Subsystem for Linux. It is one of the coolest pieces of tech I've seen since I started using Docker.
None of this code came from the Linux kernel, it was all re-implemented by Windows engineers. This is truly mind blowing.

One of the first things I did in WSL was run a syscall fuzzer. I knew it would break but it was interesting for the purposes of figuring out which syscalls had been implemented without looking at the source.
Imagine a Linux setup where you can launch PowerPoint without a VM…. well this is it!!
Obviously the unix way of 'everything is a file' does not scale well when saving a lot of small files is super slow.
posts.key_insight: Windows Subsystem for Linux represents a remarkable engineering achievement where Microsoft engineers reimplemented Linux syscalls from scratch, creating a native Linux compatibility layer that rivals containerization in its technical elegance.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle walks through her home lab setup, detailing how she rebuilt her home networking using Ubiquiti Unifi equipment and Intel NUCs. She containerized the Unifi controller, set up VLANs for network isolation, and configured SSH authentication using Yubikeys for secure remote access. The result is a setup that lets her SSH into her home lab from anywhere, including from a Pixelbook at 39,000 feet using hardware-backed authentication.
key_points: Used Ubiquiti Unifi equipment (AP, switch, security gateway) for home networking
Containerized the Unifi controller instead of buying dedicated hardware
Set up VLANs to isolate different network segments
Uses Yubikeys for SSH authentication with touch-to-authenticate enabled
Created sshb0t to sync GitHub SSH keys to server authorized_keys
Prefers minimal Chromebook setup with SSH to remote machines over local development
money_quotes: ssh-ed into my dev NUC from a Pixelbook 39,000 feet, authenticated from an ssh key on a yubikey, the future is dope AF

I always have some random side project I am working on, whether it is making the world's most over engineered desktop OS all running in containers or updating all my Makefiles to be the definition of glittering beauty.
Also be careful not to subnet yourself into a hole ;)
No, I don't use Crouton on my Chromebooks I just ssh to the cloud or to my home lab. I like things clean and minimal if you have not noticed already.
And honestly if someone gets into my GitHub account I am going to have wayyyy worse issues that them getting into my NUCs.
posts.key_insight: A well-designed home lab with proper network segmentation and hardware-backed authentication enables secure, seamless remote development from anywhere.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle details her extensive personal infrastructure setup, which includes self-hosted Jenkins CI, a private Docker registry with vulnerability scanning, a private git server, and various automation bots. She runs everything in Docker containers across NUCs and cloud instances, with automated pipelines that generate their own automation. The post serves as a practical guide to the tools and configurations she uses, demonstrating her philosophy of building custom infrastructure for control and performance.
key_points: Self-hosted Jenkins CI with DSL plugins that automatically generate configurations for new repos
Private Docker registry with Notary for signing, Clair for CVE scanning, and custom UI
Private git server with Gitiles UI, mirroring all GitHub repositories
ghb0t automatically cleans up fork branches after PRs are merged
Everything runs in Docker containers with configs available as open source Dockerfiles
money_quotes: Yep I even generate the automation…
It's super minimal and less gross than literally every other postfix image in existence.
Well because about 4 years ago when I started using docker, Docker Hub was super slow and I came to love having my own super fast one.
I am so OCD about keeping git repos clean and this is my little helper.
I run a lot of little shitty services for a personal pastebin and other things but those are all really less cool.
My attention span for blog posts is about 5 minutes and we have runneth over so I am going to call it a day with this…
posts.key_insight: Building and maintaining your own personal infrastructure gives you control, performance, and the satisfaction of automation that generates its own automation.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle takes a Cloudflare blog post about using BINFMT_MISC to script in Go and extends it to run any language through containers. She combines her binctr project (which embeds container images into static binaries) with BINFMT_MISC to execute Common Lisp scripts in isolated containers without installing anything on the host. The result is a proof-of-concept where any script can be 'dot slashed' and run in its own container environment.
key_points: BINFMT_MISC lets you register custom binary formats in the Linux kernel
The binctr project embeds entire container images into static executables
Combining these allows running any language's scripts in containers without host installation
This approach provides isolation without needing a container daemon or root privileges
money_quotes: Right, so I want to do this with *all* languages… but what I also hate is installing shit on my host. Ew, we have containers for those silly things.
It creates fully static, unprivileged, self-contained, containers as executable binaries.
So you get containers without a daemon or privileges and without even having the image for the rootfs of the container. You just need this one binary.
Imagine if an entire OS had all the languages packaged this way so that everything could be 'dot slashed' and executed but without actually installing the language to your host operating system.
Well I could do this with *any language* and I'm a bit insane haven't you noticed…
posts.key_insight: By combining kernel-level binary format registration with self-contained container binaries, you can create a system where any programming language can be executed as a script without polluting your host with dependencies.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle documents her journey to create 'img', an unprivileged container image builder for Kubernetes, after a challenge from a coworker who claimed it couldn't be done. She builds on prior art from Docker colleagues and the buildkit project, then systematically works through the technical obstacles of running unprivileged builds inside containers—requiring patches to runc, Docker, and Kubernetes itself. The post demonstrates how achieving seemingly simple goals in the container ecosystem often requires deep yak-shaving across multiple layers of the stack.
key_points: Created 'img' as an unprivileged container image builder by building on buildkit and prior Docker work
Unprivileged container builds require subuid/subgid maps, setgroups, and unprivileged mounting from inside user namespaces
Docker's default proc masking prevents mounting proc inside containers, requiring a new 'rawProc' option
The solution required patches across runc, Docker, and Kubernetes to work end-to-end
Security relies on user isolation, seccomp profiles, and masked/readonly proc in nested containers
For untrusted builds from the internet, VM-based isolation (like Intel Clear Containers) is recommended
money_quotes: Bullshit, if it was easy someone would have done it already. I've wanted this for years. Please pass the ranch dressing.
I didn't want to reinvent the world I just wanted to make it unprivileged and a single binary with a simple user interface that could easily be switched out with docker.
Not all of my ideas are good. I first started on a FUSE snapshotter. Turns out FUSE kinda sucks…
This is much better than the current situation where people are mounting the docker socket into containers and everything is running as root.
If you are running a cluster inside your organization, it's unlikely someone is going waste a kernel 0day popping your cluster from within your org.
And that ends the most epic yak shave ever, minus the patches all being merged upstream.
posts.key_insight: Building unprivileged container images in Kubernetes is achievable but requires coordinated patches across multiple layers of the container stack—from runc to Docker to Kubernetes—demonstrating how deeply intertwined these systems have become.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle argues that achieving hard multi-tenancy in Kubernetes requires more than one layer of security, drawing parallels to how operating system kernels handle privilege separation. She proposes applying 'intra-kernel isolation' to Kubernetes, where each namespace gets its own nested Kubernetes system services (api-server, kube-proxy, etc.), so that exploiting one tenant's services doesn't compromise the entire cluster. The post outlines how combining namespace isolation with node-level resource control—or alternatively using VM-based container isolation—can provide defense in depth against both API vulnerabilities and container breakouts.
key_points: Current hard multi-tenancy proposals are insufficient because a single Kubernetes exploit leads to full cluster compromise
True sandboxes require multiple overlapping security layers—not just one boundary
Kubernetes namespaces should work like Solaris Zones or FreeBSD Jails, automatically providing full isolation
Each tenant namespace should get its own nested Kubernetes control plane components
Resource isolation should extend to node assignment, preventing tenants from sharing machines
VM-based containers (like katacontainers) can provide hardware-level isolation for better resource utilization
money_quotes: Kubernetes is the new kernel. We can refer to it as a 'cluster kernel' versus the typical operating system kernel.
All systems have weaknesses and nothing is perfect. With a system as complex and large as Kubernetes it is hard to trust the entire system to not be vulnerable.
One single exploit in Kubernetes leads to full supervisor privileges and then it's game over. This is not an acceptable way to secure a system and guarantee isolation between tenants.

These layers ensure that one vulnerability in the system does not take out the entire security of the system. We need this for hard multi-tenancy in Kubernetes as well. This is why all the existing proposals are insufficient.
The namespaces in Kubernetes should automatically set up a well isolated world, just like that of Zones or Jails without the user having to worry about if they configured it correctly.
Kubernetes by default shares everything and has many different, sometimes very broken, drivers and plugins just like that of an operating system kernel.
posts.key_insight: Hard multi-tenancy in Kubernetes requires treating it like an operating system kernel and applying proven intra-kernel isolation techniques, giving each tenant their own nested control plane rather than relying on a single shared system that can be fully compromised by one exploit.

posts.summary: Frazelle pushes back against marketing claims that existing container security mechanisms are inadequate, pointing out that she personally implemented Docker's default Seccomp and Apparmor profiles which provide multiple overlapping layers of protection. She argues that gvisor's marketing dismisses this work while spreading FUD, and that large organizations suffer from 'not invented here' syndrome combined with echo chambers that prevent them from recognizing external contributions. The post defends the extensive testing and thoughtful defaults built into container runtimes while questioning whether gvisor actually provides better security given its operational complexity and larger syscall surface.
key_points: Container security relies on multiple overlapping layers: Seccomp, Apparmor, namespaces, cgroups, capabilities, and unprivileged users
The mount syscall alone is blocked by three separate security mechanisms demonstrating defense in depth
Default security profiles were exhaustively tested against every Dockerfile on GitHub before release
Large organizations suffer from 'not invented here' syndrome and echo chambers that dismiss external security work
gvisor's user-space syscall reimplementation adds operational burden and may actually use more syscalls than standard containers
money_quotes: Greetings. Reporting for duty. Literally I am the person who can do that. I was the person who did do that.

To be truly secure you need more than one layer of security so that when there is a vulnerability in one layer, the attacker also needs a vulnerability in another layer to bypass the isolation mechanism.
The tech never sells itself. It's all about marketing.

When you work at a large organization you are surrounded by an echo chamber. So if everyone in the org is saying 'containers are not secure,' you are bound to believe it and not research actual facts.
Spreading FUD while ignorant or not doing proper research is harmful to the facts and hard work many people put in to making containers at least decently isolated by default.
I truly believe that people choosing projects to use should research into them and not just choose something shiny that came out of Big Corp.
posts.key_insight: Marketing hype from large organizations often dismisses existing security work without proper research, creating echo chambers that spread FUD rather than building on the defense-in-depth mechanisms already implemented in container runtimes.

posts.summary: This is a curated index page listing Jess Frazelle's conference talks from 2015-2019, organized chronologically. The talks span topics including container security, building containers from Linux primitives, Intel SGX, open source firmware, running desktop apps in containers, and the intersection of individual contributors and corporate interests in open source. It serves as a reference archive for her public speaking on containers, security, and infrastructure topics.
key_points: Comprehensive archive of talks spanning 2015-2019 covering containers, security, firmware, and Linux internals
Topics evolved from Docker/container basics to deeper security concerns like SGX and open source firmware
Talks demonstrate hands-on approach with live demos of desktop apps in containers and building containers from primitives
Addresses both technical depth (seccomp, namespaces, cgroups) and industry dynamics (corporate vs individual contributors)
money_quotes: Open source projects strive to be transparent in everything they do, but when it comes to fixing security patches they need to find the right balance of 'open' and 'responsible.'
What is needed for containers to provide this promise? Can it be done by default? What steps are already being made to get towards containers that actually 'contain'?
This talk covers intricacies of 'choosing your battles' and how personal passion for a project might conflict with corporate motives.
The most well known sandbox is Chrome, for providing 'hard guarantees about what ultimately a piece of code can or cannot do no matter what its inputs are'.
posts.key_insight: A record of Frazelle's evolution as a speaker from container evangelism through deep-dive security research, reflecting the maturation of container technology itself from novelty to production security concerns.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle demonstrates how to install and use Wireguard VPN entirely through containers, staying true to her philosophy of never installing software directly on the host system. She provides Dockerfiles for both installing the Wireguard kernel module and running the wg command-line tools, showing how containers can manage even kernel-level components. The post includes a complete walkthrough of setting up a Wireguard connection using the demo server, with an update noting that Tailscale now offers an easier cross-platform solution using a Go implementation.
key_points: Wireguard integrates directly into the Linux networking stack, making it easy to clone interfaces into specific containers
Even kernel modules can be installed via containers by mounting /lib/modules and /usr/src
The wg tools can be wrapped in a bash alias that runs them from a container with appropriate capabilities
Tailscale emerged as a simpler cross-platform alternative using Go-based userspace Wireguard
money_quotes: If you are new to my blog, I HATEEEE installing things on my host. I run everything in containers.
Wireguard is a kernel module. BUT guess what, literally anything can be run in a container.
What is cool about Wireguard is it integrates into the Linux networking stack so you have a lot of power over interactions with it.
posts.key_insight: Containers can encapsulate and manage even kernel-level software like Wireguard, maintaining a clean host system while still gaining full functionality.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle pushes back against the assumption that she's a Kubernetes advocate, clarifying that the problems she's written about are often specific to Kubernetes' design choices rather than universal container orchestration challenges. She argues that many teams could build simpler, custom orchestrators using containerd directly, and that the truly hard problems (like unprivileged image building) exist independent of Kubernetes. The post is a call for pragmatism over hype-driven technology choices.
key_points: Problems often attributed to container orchestration are frequently specific to Kubernetes' architecture
Custom minimal orchestrators using containerd are viable alternatives
The hard problem in image building is unprivileged Linux builds, not Kubernetes-specific tooling
Docker-in-docker has worked reliably for CI for years
Technology choices should be driven by use case, not popularity
money_quotes: I have realized recently that a lot of people think I am just a shill for Kubernetes and I am not.
You could easily build your own orchestrator without them.
The hard problem I was solving in that post was not how to build images on Kubernetes but how to build images as an unprivileged user in Linux. That is a hard problem.
Or just run docker-in-docker, I have done this for years on my CI with absolutely no problems.
With complexity comes a steep learning curve and with a massive number of pluggable layers comes yak shaves until the end of time.
Think for yourselves, don't be sheep.
posts.key_insight: Kubernetes complexity is often unnecessary—choose the simplest tool that solves your actual problem rather than following industry hype.

posts.summary: This post walks through the complete lifecycle of a GitHub Action, from workflow file configuration to Docker container execution. Using a practical example of automatically deleting merged PR branches, Frazelle explains how GitHub triggers events, resolves action dependencies, builds Docker images, and executes scripts. The tutorial demystifies the Actions system by showing each component: workflow files, Dockerfiles, and the bash scripts that do the actual work.
key_points: GitHub Actions are triggered by events (like pull_request) and resolve a chain of dependent actions
Actions run in Docker containers defined by Dockerfiles that specify the runtime environment
The GITHUB_EVENT_PATH environment variable contains JSON data about the triggering event
Workflow files define which events trigger actions and what repositories/Docker images to use
Actions can access the GitHub API using secrets like GITHUB_TOKEN for authentication
money_quotes: The branch lingers around until the end of time and eats away at the part of my soul that likes everything to be clean.
The overall idea is scriptable GitHub but rather than do all that hand-wavy crap to try and explain I will take you through what happens when you run an Action.
Now, GitHub is like 'oh holy shit, something happened on a pull request, let me fire all ze missiles of things that happen on a pull request.'
This label defines our action name, we could have named it butts but I decided to be an adult.
A docker image is a bunch of files combined in a tarball.
posts.key_insight: GitHub Actions is essentially 'scriptable GitHub' where Docker containers run in response to repository events, with the workflow file orchestrating the trigger conditions and the Dockerfile defining the execution environment.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle celebrates the Unix pipe as a brilliant piece of software design that embodies the Unix philosophy of making programs that do one thing well and can be combined with others. She traces the concept back to Doug McIlroy's 1964 memo proposing ways to 'couple programs like garden hose.' The post argues that this design pattern enables creativity and extensibility by allowing simple tools to become building blocks for solving complex problems the original authors never anticipated.
key_points: The pipe operator allows output from one program to become input for another
The concept predates Unix, originating in Doug McIlroy's 1964 memo
Unix philosophy emphasizes doing one thing well and expecting output to become input for unknown future programs
Simple tools combined via pipes become more powerful than monolithic programs
Printing output to terminal is actually the 'special case' - redirection is the true power
money_quotes: We should have some ways of coupling programs like garden hose--screw in another segment when it becomes necessary to massage data in another way.
What I love about Unix is the philosophy of 'do one thing well' and 'expect the output of every program to become the input to another'.
When a user redirects the output of cat via a | to some other program, cat becomes so much more than what the original author intended.
The original author of cat does not even need to know about the larger plan. That is the beauty of the | it allows for solving problems by combining small, simple programs together.
I love software design that enables creativity, values simplicity, and doesn't put users in a box.
posts.key_insight: The Unix pipe represents brilliant software design because it allows simple, single-purpose programs to become building blocks for solving complex problems their original authors never imagined.

posts.summary: This design document outlines a secure multi-tenant container orchestrator for running untrusted third-party code in isolation. Frazelle argues for using Firecracker VMs instead of plain containers to allow full syscall access while maintaining security through multiple isolation layers. The design emphasizes defense in depth with locked-down host OSes, network deny-all defaults, eBPF monitoring, and explicit rejection of Kubernetes due to its complexity and large attack surface.
key_points: Use hypervisor-based isolation (Firecracker) instead of containers to allow full syscall interface while maintaining security
Multiple isolation layers so compromising one layer doesn't compromise the system
Network locked down by default with deny-all policy for ingress and egress
Kubernetes rejected as too complex with too large an attack surface for security-critical multi-tenancy
eBPF used extensively for monitoring, network isolation, and emergency vulnerability mitigation
Scheduler should avoid bin packing and optimize for image pull proximity
money_quotes: If one layer of isolation is compromised, rely on another layer of isolation entirely. If two layers are compromised then we at least tried our best…
I don't want the security of something that allows arbitrary code execution to have only one layer of security which someone might inadvertently turn off.
Kubernetes is hard to secure… there are a lot of components and there is no isolation between etcd, and the kubelet to apiserver communication cannot be isolated either.
Even Google doesn't use Kubernetes internally to schedule VMs, that is a whole separate thing.
If we are running on bare metal we need to account for power management, BIOS updates, hardware failures and more. These are all things the orchestration tools of today completely ignore.
By keeping our implementation more simple it is easier to keep track of the components' communication and ensure it is secure. The surface area is WAYYY smaller.
posts.key_insight: True multi-tenant security for arbitrary code execution requires defense in depth with VM-level isolation, and Kubernetes' complexity makes it unsuitable for high-security multi-tenancy despite its popularity.

posts.summary: Frazelle documents her deep dive into firmware and hardware security during her unemployment, exploring baseboard management controllers (BMC), Intel Management Engine, and Intel SGX. She traces her path from openbmc and u-bmc projects through the security vulnerabilities of closed-source firmware, ultimately landing on SGX's extensive attack surface including Foreshadow and cache attacks. The post reveals how technologies marketed as security features often contain hidden complexities, licensing restrictions, and fundamental vulnerabilities that undermine their stated purpose.
key_points: BMC projects like openbmc and u-bmc are modernizing hardware management, with u-bmc using gRPC instead of legacy IPMI
Intel Management Engine has numerous security vulnerabilities and potential backdoors, leading vendors like Dell and Purism to disable or erase it
SGX secure enclaves require trusting significant base code and are vulnerable to side-channel attacks like Foreshadow
SGX launch control originally required Intel licensing, creating market lock-in disguised as a security feature
Foreshadow-NG can access all cached memory, not just virtual memory, completely bypassing isolation guarantees
SGX can actually be weaponized to conceal and amplify cache attacks, turning a security feature into a malware toolkit
money_quotes: So in between bouncing back and forth reading about various forms of firmware and how shitty and sketchy closed source firmware is, I read the book Bad Blood... so everything from here on out is with 'paranoid as fuck' goggles on because I was shook.

My opinion, which I'm sure the readers on Hacker News will call me all sorts of names for, I question what is the point if you need to trust so much base code just to run a damn thing in the enclave and when you run your process it's slow. AND it won't even protect you from side channel attacks or timing attacks.
While SGX from the outside is a feature to secure computing, it also has this hidden feature of securing the market for Intel perhaps?
Do you need a new feature set for your malware? Because you can use SGX to conceal cache attacks and amplify them!!!
With Foreshadow-NG, the hacker can access all cached memory, not just their own virtual memory. Bananas… right. But there's more…
posts.key_insight: Hardware security features like Intel SGX are often undermined by fundamental architectural vulnerabilities and corporate licensing schemes, making their security promises more marketing than reality.

posts.summary: Frazelle argues that the end of Moore's Law, rather than being a crisis, opens up a new golden age for computer architecture and passionate building. Drawing inspiration from 'The Soul of a New Machine' and the garage-computer-building era, she contrasts the soul-driven work of small, mission-aligned teams with today's fragmented corporate engineering roles. She sees democratization of hardware design through open source tools as enabling a return to that era of building with passion and purpose.
key_points: Moore's Law ending creates opportunities rather than problems for computing innovation
The garage-era of computing was defined by small teams building with passion and soul
Modern corporate engineering often disconnects workers from the larger vision
Open source hardware tools will democratize chip design by 2030
Trust and empowerment enable people to achieve what experts think is impossible
