money_quotes: In today's age, at large companies, most engineers take jobs where they work on one teeny aspect of a machine or website or app. Sometimes they are not even aware of the larger goal or vision but just their own little world.
The team wasn't driven by power or greed, but by accomplishment and self-fulfillment. They put a part of themselves in the machine therefore producing a machine with a soul.
It's amazing what a person can do when they don't know something is impossible and are empowered to take on a task.

I think there is a different class of programmer... the idealists_dreamers? Those who build things w soul, value accomplishments & being a part of something bigger than themselves. I feel like we've lost some of that.

The end of Dennard scaling and Moore's Law and the deceleration of performance gains for standard microprocessors are not problems that must be solved but facts that, recognized, offer breathtaking opportunities.
Hardware startups will flourish for the reasons that the open source software ecosystem paired with commoditized cloud compute has unleashed software startups over the past decade.
posts.key_insight: The end of Moore's Law combined with open source hardware tools creates an opportunity to return to building computers with soul—small teams driven by passion and accomplishment rather than corporate fragmentation.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle professes her love for LD_PRELOAD, an environment variable that allows overriding library symbols by loading custom shared objects before any other library. She clarifies that LD_PRELOAD is NOT a linker flag (despite common confusion), then showcases creative uses collected from Twitter, including hacking Quake 3, bypassing Dropbox filesystem restrictions, and reproducing SSH key vulnerabilities. The post culminates in her satirical 'Cloud Native Syscalls' project that uses LD_PRELOAD to forward all syscalls to a remote cloud daemon.
key_points: LD_PRELOAD is an environment variable, not a linker flag - it loads specified shared objects before any other library including libc
This allows overriding any library function by providing your own implementation
Creative uses include game cheating, bypassing application restrictions, security research, and adding features to interpreters
The satirical 'kernelless.cloud' project demonstrates forwarding syscalls to a remote daemon via LD_PRELOAD
money_quotes: I am going to take the time right now to make one thing very clear, VERY CLEAR, listen closely: LD_PRELOAD IS NOT A FLAG. It is an environment variable. Wake up sheeple!
LD_PRELOAD allows you to override symbols in any library by specifying your new function in a shared object.
FEEL THE POWER!
F'king nuts right… I know. We are working on our A-round don't worry. It's truly revolutionary.
This is how most of my tweets start for what it's worth.
posts.key_insight: LD_PRELOAD is an incredibly powerful Linux mechanism for intercepting and replacing library functions, enabling everything from debugging and security research to creative hacks and satirical cloud architectures.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle revisits her earlier critique of Intel SGX after learning it was originally designed for DRM, not as a general execution environment. She acknowledges the HAVEN paper's novelty in repurposing enclaves for code execution and praises Joanna Rutkowska's thoughtful approach to SGX tradeoffs at Golem. The post emphasizes the importance of understanding a technology's original design intent before judging its limitations, and reflects on how users inevitably push technologies beyond their intended purposes.
key_points: SGX was originally built for DRM use cases, not general code execution
The HAVEN paper was truly novel in proposing enclaves as execution environments
Understanding original design intent makes apparent limitations more forgivable
Users will always push technologies beyond their intended purposes
Computing is fundamentally about understanding and accepting tradeoffs
money_quotes: Having this context makes the problems that arise when you try to do code execution inside an enclave seem like a forgivable sin.
The idea of executing code in an environment with encrypted memory as a way to use the cloud without trusting the cloud is a respectable feat.
Computing is all about tradeoffs.
I learned from experience with everything I've worked on that people will use it for things it was not built for. This happened a lot with Docker.
It's always fun to see the new ways people use what you build and then to iterate considering the new use cases.
posts.key_insight: Technologies should be evaluated in the context of their original design intent, and what appears to be a flaw may simply be users pushing the technology into use cases it was never built to handle.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle shares her college entrance essay to encourage others who may have been criticized for their unconventional writing style. She recounts how her satirical senior thesis about the moon landing being fake earned her first D, but she refused to compromise her authentic voice. The essay itself, 'Pharmy Tales,' is a collection of humorous vignettes from her high school job at a pharmacy, showcasing her natural storytelling ability and sardonic wit.
key_points: Received first D on AP English senior thesis for writing satirically about moon landing conspiracies
Her mom thought the unconventional pharmacy essay would prevent college acceptance; her dad loved it
Got accepted to NYU early admission despite (or because of) her authentic writing style
The pharmacy job provided endless material for storytelling
money_quotes: I like to think that I write how I speak. This was not always taken well, however. I tend to be a bit of a sarcastic troll.
Never be ashamed to be yourself and think differently.
I also value hearing all perspectives and then finding the truth somewhere in the middle.
Other than a large dose of feeling like shit (I am a perfectionist) I decided to not give any fucks.
Don't be fooled by the misconception that working in a pharmacy is dull. Everyday, I come home with a new story to tell.
posts.key_insight: Authentic self-expression and unconventional thinking, even when criticized by authority figures, can be a strength rather than a liability.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle shares her curated reading list divided into 'romanticized tech' books that capture the idealistic spirit of computing history, and non-fiction spanning leadership, science, and self-improvement. The romanticized tech section features books about Data General, CERN, Commodore, and the origins of the Internet that remind readers why they entered the field. The non-fiction recommendations range from technical resources like Google's datacenter design to management guides and physics texts, reflecting her diverse interests in leadership, mathematics, and particle physics.
key_points: 'Romanticized tech' books serve as antidotes to Black Mirror pessimism about technology
Soul of a New Machine, recommended by Bryan Cantrill, exemplifies passion-driven engineering
Management and leadership books emphasize transparency, humility, and data-driven approaches
Strong interest in physics and mathematics alongside software engineering
Book recommendations often come from her professional network (Cantrill, Lara Hogan, Nicole Forsgren)
money_quotes: I call this genre of books 'romanticized tech' because of the way tech is portrayed in them in a very idealistic and whimsical way. It's nice to pick up one of these if you are feeling very 'Black Mirror' to remember why you might have even started in this field.
I found this book at the infamous Bell's books in Palo Alto. It was all worn like someone had previously loved it a lot and it makes me love it even more.
Honestly, after reading it the world will be a much different place.
Ego so often gets in the way of great leadership and success and I greatly enjoyed reading a book that focused on self-confidence without ego.
It really details a great approach for doing so that feels like it could almost be weaponized :).
Every single book list should include Camille's book. It is a great read for managers and non-managers and has given me the tools for knowing what is normal and what is not.
posts.key_insight: Reading romanticized accounts of computing history can rekindle the passion and idealism that originally drew engineers to the field, serving as a counterbalance to tech cynicism.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle documents her experiences shadowing professionals in government (Pentagon/US Digital Service) and medicine (surgical residency) to compare their organizational cultures with tech. She finds that both fields have hierarchical structures with meaningful lessons: military badges reward actual accomplishments rather than political climbing, and attending physicians work collaboratively with residents rather than dictating from above. She contrasts these healthier dynamics with tech's dysfunction around meaningless titles and managers who hoard technical decisions instead of empowering their teams.
key_points: Military badge systems track real accomplishments rather than conferring arbitrary power
The US Digital Service operates at colonel-level rank with Secretary of State authority to cut through bureaucracy
Attending physicians work as collaborative peers with residents, 'passing the scalpel' rather than taking charge
Tech managers often fail by arriving at solutions themselves rather than letting teams solve problems
Open source collaboration models could transform government (laws as pull requests) and medicine (shared research)
money_quotes: I believe that most titles are bullshit, climbing a career ladder is bullshit, what really matters is what you do and what impact you have.
This is how leadership should work, not with power over someone else but working with others as peers and colleagues.
Wouldn't it be cool if that's how every part of the government worked? Then, if you wanted to change a process or law you would just send a pull request…

I wish more managers would focus on managing and growing the people on their team versus using it as a position of power over the technical work. If they still need to do technical work in that role it should be like that of 'passing the scalpel' when someone needs it.
I know from experience how demotivating it is seeing a shithead consistently fail up.
posts.key_insight: Functional hierarchies in government and medicine reward actual accomplishments and enable collaborative leadership, while tech often conflates titles with power and promotes managers who hoard decisions instead of empowering teams.

posts.summary: Frazelle reflects on trust and integrity in leadership after spending time observing the finance industry. She argues that transparency and trust go hand-in-hand, and leaders must earn trust through a track record of doing the right thing rather than assuming it will be given. She criticizes leaders who weaponize information asymmetry against their reports, and emphasizes that unlike respect, trust starts at zero and must be actively built.
key_points: Trust and transparency are inseparable leadership qualities
Leaders often weaponize information asymmetry against their teams
Trust starts at zero and must be earned, unlike respect
Bad experiences teach you to read people and protect yourself
'Assume good intentions' has limits based on lived experience
money_quotes: I became better at reading people after having a few really bad experiences where I should have known better than to trust someone.

Most people will tell you to always assume good intentions, and this is true to an extent. However, having been through some really bad experiences where I did 'assume good intentions' and should not have, I tend to be less and less willing to do that.
I've seen a lot of people in positions of power, use their power of keeping information private against those under them.
They don't fully disclose the 'why' and it leads to people they manage not fully being able to help solve the problem as well as not fully understanding the problem.
To gain trust, I believe a leader must also have integrity and a track record of doing the right thing.
posts.key_insight: Trust is not given but earned through consistent integrity, and leaders who default to secrecy over transparency are often weaponizing information asymmetry against their own teams.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle recaps her recent adventures exploring cutting-edge open source hardware and firmware initiatives, from seeing unikernels boot on RISC-V at Cambridge to witnessing Facebook's open firmware efforts at Open Compute Summit. She highlights the exciting progress in replacing proprietary firmware with open alternatives like LinuxBoot and Coreboot, while critiquing Intel's Boot Guard for locking users out of their own hardware. The post serves as a travelogue through the frontier of open hardware innovation during her time between jobs.
key_points: MirageOS team at Cambridge is booting unikernels on bare-metal RISC-V, enabling more secure minimal environments
Facebook runs LinuxBoot, Coreboot, and OpenBMC on their infrastructure to replace proprietary firmware
Intel's Boot Guard only allows Intel-signed firmware, preventing users from running open alternatives
RISC-V cloud provider support is likely years away but would accelerate ecosystem adoption
money_quotes: Honestly, unikernels replacing all the crap firmware in the world would be a huge win.
If a person owns the hardware, they have a right to own the firmware as well. Boot Guard prevents this.
This 'feature' from hardware vendors is preventing the innovation of this community and preventing pushing technology to a safer place.
If you are in a position to push back on these hardware vendors, please do so. They need all the help they can get.
The Facebook rack and node designs are seriously gorgeous, simple.
posts.key_insight: Hardware ownership should include firmware ownership, and the open firmware movement is making real progress despite vendor lock-in mechanisms like Intel's Boot Guard.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle defines what a distinguished engineer should embody after experiencing the 'N+1 shithead problem' - being demotivated by seeing senior engineers behave poorly. She outlines a comprehensive framework covering technical leadership qualities like empowering others, giving constructive criticism, having opinions loosely held, and prioritizing shipping. The post emphasizes that distinguished engineers must also engage with external communities, remain humble, and ultimately demonstrate these values through actions rather than words.
key_points: Distinguished engineers should empower colleagues to tackle challenges beyond their comfort zone
Having 'opinions loosely held' rather than 'strong opinions, loosely held' prevents overpowering junior voices
Technical leaders must be bridges between teams with strong communication skills
Learning from external communities prevents echo chamber effects and Dunning-Kruger-like blind spots
Shipping and decisiveness matter, but not at the cost of burning out teams
Actions speak louder than words - you must actually do these things
money_quotes: The 'N+1 shithead problem' occurs when you are demotivated by seeing people who are a level above you behave poorly, or more bluntly when they behave like a shithead.
Why would I want to be at their level, when once I get there I'll just be one amongst the dipshits.
I personally believe you don't need a high title to take on a hard task, you just need the support and faith that you are capable of handling it.
Making an active effort not to say 'strong opinions, loosely held' because with a power dynamic that could over power the rest of the voices.
If you silo yourself to only learning within your company, you are missing out on a world of experiences and expertise different than yours from the external community.
Most importantly you must actually do these things. Actions speak louder than words.
posts.key_insight: A distinguished engineer's value lies not in technical brilliance alone, but in their ability to empower others, remain humble, and model the behavior that makes junior engineers want to reach that level.

posts.summary: Frazelle uses her exploration of RISC-V as a case study for how she learns new technical topics. Rather than diving straight into RISC-V specifics, she recursively digs into foundational concepts like instruction set architectures, examining RISC, CISC, VLIW, and other ISA types first. She reviews the Berkeley RISC-V design paper, highlighting why existing proprietary ISAs like x86 (with ~2,500 instructions) and ARM were unsuitable, and why an open, simpler architecture was needed. The post deliberately ends before covering RISC-V's actual design to illustrate that deep learning requires extensive foundational exploration.
key_points: Learning technique involves recursively digging deeper into foundational concepts before tackling the main topic
RISC-V is a free and open instruction set architecture, unlike proprietary alternatives like x86 and ARM
Existing ISAs are overly complex (x86 has ~2,500 instructions, ARMv7 has 600+) or have technical limitations
ISAs can die (like Alpha), making open standards important for longevity
Self-belief and high expectations are key to learning anything new
money_quotes: I think anyone is capable of doing or learning anything, they just need the right motivation and to believe in themselves.
This is my learning technique. I bounce from one thing to another, recursively digging deeper as I learn more.
You cannot get a hard copy of the x86 manual anymore and even in PDF form it's ~5,000 pages and that doesn't include the extensions. Who has time to read all of that?
Your own learning is almost like a choose your own adventure game. You can choose to go further down any path at any time.
Look how much I dug into the fundamentals of instruction sets before even digging into the thing I set out to learn.
posts.key_insight: Deep technical learning requires embracing a recursive process of exploring foundational concepts before tackling your actual goal, treating it like a choose-your-own-adventure game where you dig into whatever sparks curiosity.

posts.summary: Frazelle applies Conway's Law to the software stack and identifies a critical problem: insufficient communication between hardware, firmware, kernel, and application layers leads to dangerous security assumptions. She provides examples from hardware engineers who never intended chips for multi-tenant isolation (leading to Spectre/Meltdown), firmware complexity that frustrates kernel developers, and Kubernetes being misused for multi-tenant isolation despite not being designed for it. The post questions how the industry can solve this fundamental disconnect between stack layers.
key_points: Hardware was never designed for multi-tenant isolation, which is the fundamental premise of cloud computing
Firmware complexity from CPU vendors creates bugs that kernel developers cannot debug or control
Kubernetes security features are often 'window dressing' that can be bypassed with deeper stack knowledge
The industry lacks mechanisms to promote communication between different layers of the software stack
money_quotes: All of the hardware engineers either laugh or are horrified and the resounding reaction is 'you'd be crazy to think hardware was ever intended to be used for isolating multiple users safely.'
Speculative execution was a feature intended to make processors faster but was never thought about in terms of the vector of hacking something running multi-tenant compute, like a cloud provider.
All the complexity in the firmware leads to overlooked bugs and odd behavior that can't be controlled or debugged from the kernel developers layer and/or user space.
I was horrified to find people are running multi-tenant kubernetes clusters with multiple customers processes, aka for isolating untrusted processes.
They are people thinking an interface or feature is secure when it is merely a window dressing that can be bypassed with just a bit more knowledge about the stack.
Or is the answer simply, own all the layers of the stack yourself?
posts.key_insight: The entire cloud computing industry is built on dangerous assumptions because different layers of the stack—from hardware to orchestration—were designed in isolation without understanding how they would be composed for multi-tenant security.

posts.summary: Frazelle reflects on spending a day shadowing investigative journalist Jeff Kao and discovering parallels between journalism and her technical background. She explores how software engineering tools like diffs and version control could serve as mechanisms for transparency and truth-finding in journalism and governance. The post draws connections between text comparison techniques used in investigative pieces (like uncovering fake net neutrality comments) and the everyday tools of source control.
key_points: Investigative journalism shares methodological similarities with academic research, but is driven more by intuition
Diffs and version control could serve as tools for transparency in journalism and law
Text comparison techniques have been used to uncover corporate influence on legislation and fake public comments
Separating emotion from truth in news is a critical challenge in the current media landscape
money_quotes: Humans are individual snowflakes and no one is exactly like the next. It is our unique experiences that form the way we think and behave, as well as what drives us.
Investigative journalism seems to be driven by intuition, while academia might be more driven by novel research.
With a diff, the history of a document is fully transparent, anyone can see any and all changes to it.
So much of the news today is trying to trigger an emotional response for clicks. Or in the worst case, it is trying to trigger an emotional response for influencing an election.
The truth itself should be enough of a trigger.
posts.key_insight: The technical tools engineers use daily for tracking changes and finding differences—like version control diffs—could be powerful instruments for transparency and truth-seeking in journalism and governance.

posts.summary: The post proposes a 'Leadership CI' framework—a set of self-assessment questions leaders should ask themselves before making decisions, analogous to how software uses continuous integration tests before merging code. Co-authored with Kathy Simpson, it argues that leaders need pre-established checks to avoid decisions driven by fear and pride, instead favoring growth, humility, and inclusion. The framework includes questions about ego, transparency, curiosity, manipulation, and whether you're empowering your team or creating an echo chamber.
key_points: Leaders need systematic checks before decisions, like CI tests for code
Fear and pride lead to devastating consequences; growth and humility lead to better outcomes
Key tests include: transparency, inclusion, curiosity, and whether your team feels safe disagreeing
The framework should evolve over time as new situations arise
Self-serving decisions fail the test; morally good decisions with long-term impact pass
money_quotes: There are devastating consequences to taking actions based on fear and pride; we hope this set of questions enables taking action based on growth, humility, inclusion, and soulful reflection.

As leaders, before acting or making decisions it would be nice to have a set of tests and checks, established ahead of time, to make sure we are in the right headspace to think, behave and make decisions that are in the best interest of everyone and our company.
Pass: I'm asking questions that make me uncomfortable, and I'm comfortable being wrong. Fail: I want my way.

Pass: They freely and continually come to me with answers and information that they know I will not like. Fail: They go to each other or people outside the team with the information, and telling me what they think I want to hear.
Pass: I am doing the minority of the speaking and people are disagreeing with my opinions. Fail: I am being quoted back to myself.
posts.key_insight: Just as software engineers use CI to catch bugs before they ship, leaders should use a personal checklist of introspective questions to catch ego-driven or fear-based decisions before they cause harm.

posts.summary: Frazelle outlines three critical questions enterprises should ask before adopting cloud services. She emphasizes cutting through hype to understand actual need, demanding transparency about internal implementation and security practices, and verifying that features solve real customer problems rather than product manager hunches. The post reflects deep skepticism about cloud providers' security posture and transparency.
key_points: Determine if you actually need a cloud product before buying into the hype
Cloud providers should be able to articulate their own threat model and implementation details
Service agreements should clearly define disclosure requirements for breaches and vulnerabilities
Features built 'for fun' or on PM feelings rather than customer needs are warning signs
Open source implementations are the exception, not the rule, in cloud infrastructure
money_quotes: So much of the cloud is built on popsicle sticks and glue.
Does that make you feel safe at night knowing your customer data is being stored in a proof of concept that was shipped before it should have been?
What if they don't know if they were hacked after a vulnerability is public? Red flag…
If they themselves do not know their own threat model, that should be a huge warning sign.
So often these features seem to be built for fun or based off a feeling a product manager had.
posts.key_insight: Before trusting a cloud provider with your data, demand to understand their internal threat model and implementation quality—if they can't answer these questions, that's your answer.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle tests Transposit, a SQL interface for APIs, by rebuilding her 'Gitable' bot that syncs GitHub issues to Airtable. She walks through replacing complex 'glue code' and N+1 query problems with simple SQL joins across APIs. Despite attempting to break the platform with her 'weirdest' bot, the tool handles the complexity gracefully, including pagination and API rate limiting.
key_points: Transposit provides a SQL interface for interacting with multiple APIs simultaneously
The N+1 query problem in API glue code can be solved with SQL joins
Built-in API caching helps avoid rate limiting issues
Complex bot logic involving multiple APIs can be simplified significantly
SQL and Javascript operations can be combined for flexible workflows
money_quotes: Usually when I have a complex bot I end up writing a lot of 'glue code' to combine a few different APIs and get the information I want.
I fucking love Airtable. It's design just feels right and works the way my brain works.
Many APIs don't even have graphQL interfaces but also I am old school and I don't really want to learn something new.
Okay so now's the part where I am thinking… I'm going to break this thing. (Narrator: I didn't.)
It also caches the response which is a huge win because I am the queen of being rate limited.
posts.key_insight: Complex API integration code with N+1 problems and pagination logic can be dramatically simplified using a SQL abstraction layer that handles the optimization automatically.

posts.summary: The post examines the security implications of proprietary firmware running at the most privileged levels of computing hardware (Rings -2 and -3), which paradoxically have the least visibility despite having more access than the operating system or hypervisor. Frazelle explains how the Intel Management Engine and UEFI kernel contain hidden kernels with their own networking stacks and web servers, creating massive attack surfaces. She advocates for open source firmware initiatives like linuxboot, coreboot, and the NERF stack as solutions to improve security through transparency and reduced complexity.
key_points: Rings -2 and -3 (SMM, UEFI, Management Engine) have more privileges than the OS kernel but are completely proprietary and opaque
The Intel Management Engine runs Minix 3, has a web server and full networking stack, and can turn on nodes and re-image disks invisibly
A bug in the Intel Management Engine's web server went undetected for seven years
The NERF stack and linuxboot replace proprietary firmware with hardened Linux drivers, improving both security and boot times (20x faster)
True root of trust verification is impossible without open source firmware across all hardware components
Major cloud providers have their own root of trust solutions (Cerberus, Titan, Nitro) but still assume trust in proprietary code
money_quotes: It's counter-intuitive that the code that we have the least visibility into has the most privileges. This is what open source firmware is aiming to fix.
We have very little visibility into what the code in these rings is actually doing, which is horrifying considering these rings have the most privileges.
Between Ring -2 and Ring -3 we have at least 2 and a half other kernels in our stack as well as a bunch of proprietary and unnecessary complexity.
How do we really know that the firmware in a component is not vulnerable or that is doesn't have any backdoors? Well we can't. Not unless it was all open source.
Better to use a open kernel with a lot of eyes on it, than the 2½ other kernels that were all different and closed off.
I'm surprised how many people are responding that they love DigitalOcean but seem entirely unconcerned there's no answer here. You should be concerned.
posts.key_insight: True hardware security is impossible to achieve or verify when the most privileged code in our systems remains proprietary and invisible, making open source firmware essential rather than optional.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle reflects on the often-invisible power dynamics that affect how leaders are perceived and how their communications land. She argues that people only see a limited view of leaders through brief interactions, which can lead to misunderstandings when feedback feels harsh or requests feel like demands. The post advocates for vulnerability, transparency about mistakes, and patience in building trust over time.
key_points: People only see a small subset of who leaders really are through limited communications
Power dynamics amplify how feedback and requests are received
Hero worship sets people up for disappointment since everyone is flawed
Trust is built through transparency about mistakes and growth over time
Those in power often can't see the dynamics at play from their position
money_quotes: The best we can do is apologize and grow when we fuck up.
I really do not enjoy when people hero worship me and I do not think people should hero worship anyone. We are all humans and we are all flawed in our own ways.
Leave room for people to make mistakes, because they will. What truly matters is how a person grows after making a mistake.
When someone discovers a mistake you've made try to treat it as a gift.
It's hard to see a power dynamic at play if you are in it and hold the power. Power dynamics are in the eye of the beholder.
posts.key_insight: Power dynamics distort how leaders are perceived and how their words land, making transparency about mistakes and patience in building trust essential for effective leadership.

posts.summary: This foreword for the 'Linux Observability with BPF' book explains why BPF (Berkeley Packet Filters) and XDP (Express Data Path) represent a significant advancement in Linux kernel capabilities. Drawing from her experience as a Docker core maintainer dealing with iptables pain points, Frazelle explains how BPF solves real problems in container networking, system tracing, and DDoS mitigation. She emphasizes that BPF enables developers to trace syscalls, build custom tools, and prove performance issues with hard data, all while being production-ready due to its ability to filter and aggregate at the source of events.
key_points: BPF and XDP solve real problems that plagued container infrastructure, particularly iptables limitations
BPF enables tracing of any syscall, kernel function, or user-space program
XDP is highly efficient for packet filtering because it doesn't even allocate kernel structs
BPF is production-ready because it filters and aggregates at the source rather than using lossy queues
The technology is being adopted by major players like Cloudflare, Facebook, and Cilium for DDoS protection and networking
money_quotes: No longer would my scars from iptables bleed with another bug!
Never underestimate the power of proving someone wrong with hard data.
The best tools allow autonomy in their interfaces for developers like you to use them for things even the authors never imagined.
I learned very early the difference between knowing the name of something and knowing something.

The beauty of BPF lies in the fact that before now other tools used lossy queues to send sample sets to user space for aggregation whereas, BPF is great for production since it allows for constructing histograms and filtering right at the source of events.
You won't just learn BPF you will understand it.
posts.key_insight: BPF represents a paradigm shift in Linux observability and networking by moving filtering and tracing into the kernel itself, enabling production-grade tooling that was previously impossible with tools like iptables.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle offers business executives a reality check on Kubernetes adoption, cutting through the hype to explain the significant challenges organizations face. She covers the risks of using Kubernetes for stateful data, the security dangers of exposed dashboards, the operational pain of upgrades that consistently break production, and the steep learning curve that leads to outages. Even managed Kubernetes offerings don't eliminate these problems—they still require deep expertise and carry their own isolation and security risks.
key_points: Kubernetes StatefulSets can lead to data loss or corruption requiring manual intervention, making it unsuitable for critical stateful data without proper replication
Exposed Kubernetes dashboards are a major security risk that can lead to complete infrastructure takeover, data breaches, or ransom situations
Kubernetes upgrades frequently break production systems, necessitating multiple clusters to maintain reliability
Managed Kubernetes from cloud providers doesn't eliminate the learning curve or make your team's responsibility for uptime go away
Every hour spent debugging YAML is time not spent building actual business value
money_quotes: There is a lot of hype in our field and little truth behind the hype.
If your dashboard is exposed, anyone could find your dashboard and then control it.

As with learning anything, things only get worse before they get better. So get ready for a lot of production outages and failovers as your team starts to learn the ins and outs of this overly complex system.
Hope is not a strategy.
For every line of YAML written and debugged to get your website running, it is time that is being taken away from building on what your business actually does.
Just because it's managed does not mean that your application's uptime and availability are covered. That is still on your team.
posts.key_insight: Kubernetes can deliver real benefits, but executives need to understand the significant operational complexity, security risks, and learning curve costs before adopting it—managed offerings don't eliminate these challenges, they just shift some of the burden.

posts.summary: Frazelle uses transactional memory (specifically Intel's TSX) as a case study of how tech hype can lead to flawed implementations. She explains how transactional memory trades deadlocks for livelocks and creates security vulnerabilities that enabled attacks like DrK to break KASLR without generating page faults. The post traces how the transactional memory hype originated in academia around 2008, was implemented by Intel in 2012 despite practitioner warnings, and reflects on how hype propagation channels have evolved from academic journals to social media.
key_points: Transactional memory eliminates deadlocks but introduces livelocks instead
Intel's TSX implementation aborts transactions without informing the OS, creating security blind spots
The DrK attack exploited TSX to break KASLR without generating any page faults
Practitioners like Bryan Cantrill warned against transactional memory in 2008, but Intel shipped TSX anyway in 2012
Hype channels have shifted from academic conferences and journals to social networks where everyone has a voice
money_quotes: Transactional memory removes the possibility of getting a deadlock and replaces it with what is known as a livelock.

Intel's implementation of TSX behaves in such a way that when a transaction aborts due to a hardware exception, it does not fire typical exceptions. Instead, it invokes a user-specified abort handler without informing the underlying OS. This seems like it might lead to some really bad behavior… we should probably know wtf is going on in our system at any given point in time.
Some practitioners, Bryan Cantrill and Jeff Bonwick, wrote rebuttles in the name of 'please dear god do not make transactional memory A Thing'.
Hype cycles come and go and if you spend anytime in our industry you tend to become pretty numb to them. Seeing through the hype has always been a joy of mine.

Hype seems to travel through the unconscious need of people to connect to others. Being a part of movements, like open source projects and a shared sense of need, allows people to be a part of something bigger than just themselves.

The way technology can spread has turned from a place where very few people have a voice (through getting papers accepted at conferences and in journals) to social networks where everyone has a voice.
posts.key_insight: Tech hype that originates in academia and ignores practitioner warnings can lead to flawed implementations with real security consequences, as demonstrated by Intel's TSX enabling side-channel attacks that practitioners predicted would be problematic.

posts.summary: Frazelle recounts her experience at the Open Source Firmware Conference hackathon, where she attempted to replace proprietary BMC firmware with OpenBMC on a Supermicro motherboard. The project hit numerous obstacles including SPI flash contention issues, write speed problems, and debugging without a serial console. Despite not achieving a fully working open source BMC by the end, she frames the failures as valuable learning experiences about low-level hardware debugging.
key_points: Purchased a Supermicro X10SLM-F board off eBay to run CoreBoot, starting with the BMC rather than CPU BIOS
The SPI flash development cycle is painful: 16MB requires erasing 4KB sectors at 120ms each before writing
Got locked out of SPI flash when BMC started interfacing with it, requiring desoldering and installing a qspimux multiplexer
Discovered they were writing firmware too quickly, causing data corruption that wasn't detected until comparing read-back images
The board still wouldn't boot the original firmware after all fixes, leaving the mystery unsolved
money_quotes: If you've ever used a Supermicro server I probably don't need to tell you that it's a piece of shit running a 2.6 linux kernel on the BMC.
I am going to interrupt the story here for a second to explain the pain involved with this development cycle of writing to firmware to SPI flash.
This is a huge pain in the ass. It turns out it is such a pain in the ass that people have made solutions for it.
I would have been sad had everything gone smoothly because we would not have learned as much about how to debug all the components of the SPI flash and board.

Having been pushed to attend vendor conferences and trade shows through my career for various jobs, it was so refreshing to have the chance to hang out with folks from such a genuine community that really just want to help one another.
posts.key_insight: The messy, failure-prone process of hacking on open source firmware—complete with desoldering chips and debugging SPI flash timing issues—is ultimately more educational and rewarding than a smooth success would have been.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle announces the founding of Oxide Computer Company alongside Steve Tuck and Bryan Cantrill, a venture aimed at building integrated hardware and software for on-premises infrastructure. After extensive conversations with companies running their own infrastructure, she identified a significant pain point: organizations with legitimate needs for on-premises computing (security, latency, economics) have been neglected by existing vendors. Oxide aims to bring the 'infrastructure privilege' that hyperscalers like Google and Facebook enjoy to everyone else, through better hardware-software integration, power distribution, and density.
key_points: On-premises infrastructure operators are experiencing significant pain and have been neglected by existing vendors
Organizations have legitimate reasons for on-premises: security, latency, specialized workloads, and cloud unit economics at scale
Hyperscalers have 'infrastructure privilege' from building their own integrated hardware/software stacks
Oxide aims to democratize this infrastructure privilege for everyone else
Better hardware-software integration leads to improved power distribution, density, and environmental benefits
money_quotes: Hyperscalers like Facebook, Google, and Microsoft have what I like to call 'infrastructure privilege' since they long ago decided they could build their own hardware and software to fulfill their needs better than commodity vendors.
The consensus from all my conversations has been that everyone setting up infrastructure themselves is in a great deal of pain and they have been largely neglected by any existing vendor.
We are working to bring that same infrastructure privilege to everyone else!

All these folks have very good reasons for running on premises that include security, strategic reasons like latency, specialized workloads, or the reality that the unit economics of running at their scale in the cloud are unsustainable.
In typical cliche computer company fashion we have been working out of my garage.
posts.key_insight: The gap between hyperscaler infrastructure capabilities and what's available to everyone else represents a massive opportunity to bring integrated, purpose-built hardware and software to organizations with legitimate on-premises needs.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle documents the network infrastructure setup for Oxide Computer Company's new office, treating it with the same care as her previous home lab posts. She details the UniFi-based setup including a 48-port switch, Dream Machine router, multiple access points, and an isolated secondary network for lab equipment. The post reflects the early startup phase where getting reliable internet took priority over furniture.
key_points: Prioritized network connectivity over furniture on move-in day
Used Cat7 cables with awareness of distance-speed tradeoffs
Created isolated secondary network for lab equipment using AmpliFi Alien
Built scalable infrastructure anticipating team growth
money_quotes: Much like moving into a new place, the first thing I always plan to have setup on move-in day is internet.
Everyone knows shitty internet is a productivity killer.
For the office I really wanted our network infrastructure to be off-the-charts good.
It's been fun to build out the network infrastructure in our office and make sure it scales while we scale out the team.
posts.key_insight: Building robust, scalable network infrastructure from day one is a foundational investment that reflects a startup's technical priorities and culture.

posts.summary: This post provides a comprehensive technical breakdown of data center power efficiency, explaining the differences between 'carbon neutral,' 'net zero,' and 'carbon negative' designations while examining how hyperscalers like Google, Facebook, and Microsoft optimize power usage. Frazelle dives deep into the engineering trade-offs of power distribution systems, comparing rack-level DC bus bars to individual AC power supplies, and explains how the Open Compute Project's designs achieve significant efficiency gains. The post argues that wider adoption of rack-scale architectures could reduce wasted carbon emissions across the industry, not just at hyperscale companies.
key_points: Traditional data centers waste 21-27% of power in AC-to-DC conversions and transformations
The Open Compute Project achieved 38% less energy use and 24% lower costs at Facebook's Prineville data center
48VDC bus bars require only 500A versus 2kA for 12VDC, dramatically reducing copper costs and resistive losses
PUE as a metric is flawed because it doesn't account for data center location, capacity utilization, or seasonal variations
Most companies claiming 'carbon neutral' status are buying offsets that avoid emissions rather than removing carbon they emit
money_quotes: Most companies become 'carbon neutral' by investing in offsets that primarily avoid emissions, such as paying folks to not cut down trees or buying RECs. These offsets do not actually remove the carbon that they are emitting.
If a company calls themselves 'carbon negative' it means they are removing more carbon than they emit each year. This should be the gold standard for how companies operate.
Comparing a PUE value of data centres is somewhat meaningless unless it is known whether it is operating at full capacity or not.
For every unnecessary power conversion, you are paying an efficiency cost.

Server vendors would rather carry a small number of power supply SKUs that are oversized, than carry a large number of power supply SKUs that are right-sized to purpose since the economies of scale prefer the former.
While the hyperscalers have benefitted from these gains in power efficiency, most of the industry is still waiting.
posts.key_insight: Rack-level power architectures with DC bus bars can achieve 8-12% efficiency gains over commodity servers, and broader adoption of Open Compute designs would significantly reduce the tech industry's carbon footprint beyond just hyperscalers.

posts.summary: This post traces the evolution of data storage from 1951's UNISERVO tape drives through modern NVMe and emerging technologies like HAMR and storage class memory. Using a single byte (the letter 'j') as a narrative thread, Frazelle explains how each storage medium physically represents 0s and 1s—from holes in paper cards to magnetic polarities to trapped electrons in floating gates. The piece concludes by examining cutting-edge technologies like phase change memory, helium-filled drives, and heat-assisted magnetic recording that will define storage's future.
key_points: Storage has evolved from sequential-access tape (1951) to random-access disk (1956) to modern NVMe drives that are 486,111% faster than original metal tape
The fundamental unit remains the same—storing 0s and 1s—but the physical mechanisms vary wildly: holes in cards, magnetic polarities, electron positions, phase states, and optical reflectivity
Flash memory inventor Fujio Masuoka received only $758,000 from Toshiba despite flash memory's massive industry impact
Modern storage class memory (PCM, STT-RAM, ReRAM) aims to bridge the gap between volatile RAM speed and non-volatile storage persistence
Energy-assisted magnetic recording (MAMR and HAMR) represents the frontier of hard disk technology, with HAMR requiring heating regions to 750°F in under a nanosecond
money_quotes: At this point in history, you could measure the speed of a storage algorithm by the distance the tape traveled.
Manufacturing rope memory was laborious, slow, and required skills analogous to textile work; it could take months to weave a program into the rope memory.

He sued Toshiba, demanding compensation for his work, which settled in 2006 with a one-time payment of ¥87m, equivalent to $758,000. This still seems light given how impactful flash memory has been on the industry.
If we compare NVMe to the 1951 UNISERVO metal tape, NVMe can read 486,111% more digits per second.
One thing that remains true is the storing of 0s and 1s. The means by which we do that vary greatly.
posts.key_insight: While storage technology has advanced exponentially in speed and density over 70 years, the fundamental abstraction remains unchanged: everything reduces to representing and persisting 0s and 1s through whatever physical mechanism the era's technology enables.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle argues that automation is fundamentally about reclaiming time by transferring repetitive manual tasks to scripts and services. She presents a simple equation: time gained equals time doing the task manually minus time to automate it. The post covers her personal automations (employee onboarding in Rust, Gmail filters, newsletter management) while celebrating how consumer tools like Apple Shortcuts, home automation, and IFTTT are democratizing automation patterns for non-programmers.
key_points: Automation ROI is simple: if time to automate is less than cumulative time doing the task manually, automate it
Start by making automation work first, then make it pretty - minimize time spent on the automation itself
Consumer products like Apple Shortcuts and IFTTT are bringing developer-style automation to mass audiences
Managing configurations as code (like Gmail filters) provides version control benefits like git revert
The feeling of productivity from automation should not be exclusive to programmers
money_quotes: Time is one of the most valuable resources in the world. If there was something you could do to free more time for yourself, why wouldn't you?

I, personally, feel at my best and most productive when I am building something, solving a problem, or learning something new. In none of those situations does that include 'doing something manual that could otherwise be scripted/automated away.'
I basically automated the role of CIO in Rust.
By getting the thing to work first without wasting time on making it pretty, I find I can gain the most time and be most productive.
The feeling developers get after writing a script to make their life easier should not be exclusive.
You can imagine that most of my automations start out looking like a Rube Goldberg machine.
posts.key_insight: Automation is fundamentally a time arbitrage calculation, and consumer tools are finally democratizing this productivity superpower beyond programmers.

posts.summary: As display technology advances from HD to 4K to 8K, bandwidth requirements are growing exponentially—8K streaming requires 100mbps while average US speeds barely reach 96mbps. The 'last mile' problem makes fiber deployment prohibitively expensive, as Google Fiber discovered. Satellite internet from companies like Starlink and Astranis may finally solve the connectivity gap, delivering gigabit speeds without the infrastructure challenges of laying physical cable to every home.
key_points: 8K streaming requires 100mbps, but average US download speed is only 96.25mbps
The 'last mile' problem makes fiber deployment extremely expensive
Google Fiber failed because laying new fiber lines to homes was too costly
Satellite internet (Starlink, Astranis) may bypass the last mile problem entirely
Multiple devices sharing bandwidth means households actually need multiples of recommended speeds
money_quotes: If we know that the pixel density of televisions is only going to increase over time, causing streaming services to need more bandwidth, why are we not seeing a bunch of fiber being laid down or other innovations to get faster internet to the mass market?
The 'last mile' is the last bit of cable to get the connection to your home or business, these are known as drop cables.

Fiber is similar to public infrastructure like a freeway, you need to put in the investment upfront but it will pay off dividends over time. Most companies do not get that and want an economic return upfront.
The solution to the 'last mile' problem might be wireless, which leads us to the current innovations with satellites.
As the world continues to move from physical to digital at a rapid pace, we should see high bandwidth internet take a front and center role.
posts.key_insight: Display technology is advancing faster than internet infrastructure can support it, and satellite internet may be the only economically viable path to universal high-bandwidth access.

posts.summary: Frazelle uses her mother's frustrating experience with cheap, underpowered Windows machines to argue that manufacturers shouldn't sell computers with specs so low they provide terrible user experiences. She contrasts this with Apple's approach of maintaining minimum quality standards, then pivots to an optimistic vision: emerging technologies like process migration and 'teleforking' could let even low-spec machines offload computation to remote resources seamlessly. The future of computing should make powerful compute readily available to everyone, not just those who can afford premium hardware.
key_points: Low-cost Windows machines with 4GB RAM create terrible user experiences that frustrate consumers
Apple maintains minimum quality standards that ensure longevity - an 8-year-old MacBook Pro still runs great
Process migration technologies like 'popcorn' and 'teleforking' could revolutionize how we think about local computing power
The future should allow seamless offloading of processes to remote compute when local resources are insufficient
money_quotes: It infuriates me to no end that consumers have an option of even buying a $400 computer that will give them such a terrible experience.
Manufacturers of computers should be embarrassed for even selling such a shit machine. I know I would be.
Future computers should not have limited computing power, just limited local computing power.
An overburdened laptop could fork a process to your gaming console while you were at work and vice-versa while you were playing a game.
Compute should be easily shared and readily available.
People should be able to create anything they imagine on a computer that gives them unlimited power to do so.
posts.key_insight: The solution to the epidemic of underpowered consumer computers isn't just better hardware standards—it's making compute a seamlessly shareable, distributed resource so local specs become irrelevant.

posts.summary: This post provides a comprehensive technical overview of all seven ASTM-classified additive manufacturing processes, from FDM to sheet lamination, before comparing two consumer 3D printers: the MakerBot Replicator+ (FDM) and Form 3 (SLA). Frazelle's hands-on testing reveals the Form 3 produces significantly higher quality prints in less time, though the MakerBot offers better user experience with its iPad app and camera integration. The article reflects her characteristic approach of doing deep research before purchasing, wanting 'the best' thing that 'just works' as an integrated product.
key_points: The seven ASTM-classified additive manufacturing processes each have distinct tradeoffs in materials, precision, speed, and applications
SLA (stereolithography) produces smoother, more detailed prints than FDM but traditionally had brittleness limitations
The Form 3 outperformed the MakerBot Replicator+ in both print quality and speed across all test prints
MakerBot's software experience was frustrating with clunky apps and poor cloud sync, while Form Labs' setup was smoother
Future 3D printing improvements should focus on automatic support removal and reliable quality monitoring
money_quotes: I like to do a lot of research and I tend to want the best thing.

What I was personally looking for was a product, meaning something easy to set up, easy to use, and including a fully integrated experience between the hardware and the software. I didn't want something I would have to maintain or debug since I would rather this just work.
Kinda feels like what I would expect an app written by devs with hardware expertise, rather than software expertise, would feel like.

The ability to go from a digital file to a physical object rapidly with many different materials can enable so many folks to create something they could only imagine in their wildest dreams until now.
Obviously having two 3D printers is a bit unseemly so I decided after I tried them both I would donate one to a nearby school.
posts.key_insight: When evaluating hardware products, the integrated software experience matters as much as the underlying technology, and SLA's superior print quality and speed make it the better choice for those seeking polished results over raw hackability.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle describes building an automated internal infrastructure system at Oxide Computer Company that consolidates data from various external services (Google Forms, GitHub, etc.) into a single database with an API layer on top. The system evolved from 'bash, popsicle sticks, glue, and some rust' into a cleaner Rust-based architecture that provides resilience against API rate limits and service downtime. The infrastructure handles everything from applicant tracking to RFD (Request for Discussion) document management, with Airtable serving as a visual frontend for the data.
key_points: Built a centralized database to cache data from external services, protecting against rate limits and downtime
Automated applicant tracking including document parsing, email notifications, and GitHub issue creation for onboarding
RFD content stored in database enables easy searching and sharing with external collaborators
API server acts as read-only middle-man between database and internal tools/users
Airtable used as visual frontend with automatic syncing from the database
money_quotes: I joke so much that I automated our CIO that I even named the repo holding the code…cio.
Previously, our infrastructure was held together with bash, popsicle sticks, glue, and some rust. Now, it is mostly rust and a much more sane architecture to grok.
We also get the freedom of caching all our data in a database that we own so we can access it even when services are down.
At this point, I can't imagine working at a company without an internal API for querying everything from Google groups, to applicants, to mailing list subscribers, to RFDs, and more.
All in all, this was pretty fun to build, refactor, build, and refactor again.
posts.key_insight: Owning your company's operational data in a centralized database with an API layer provides resilience, searchability, and the flexibility to build custom tooling on top of external services.

posts.summary: This post provides a comprehensive technical deep-dive into battery technology, prompted by Tesla's 2020 Battery Day announcement. Frazelle explains the fundamentals of how batteries work—from the chemistry of redox reactions to the differences between alkaline and lithium-ion cells—before exploring emerging technologies like solid-state batteries, nuclear batteries, and silicon anodes. The bulk of the post analyzes Tesla's holistic approach to battery improvement, which addresses cell design, manufacturing processes, materials selection, and vehicle integration to achieve their goal of halving cost per kilowatt-hour.
key_points: Tesla's tabless battery design reduces electrical path length from 250mm to 50mm, enabling better thermal performance and simpler manufacturing

Silicon anodes can store 9x more lithium ions than graphite but swell 3-4x during charging, causing fractures—Tesla's solution uses raw silicon with elastic polymer coatings at $1.20/kWh vs competitors' $6-100+/kWh
Tesla's dry electrode process eliminates the solvent step entirely, resulting in 10x reduction in factory footprint and energy consumption
Making the battery pack structural (like aircraft fuel tanks in wings) reduces body mass by 10% and increases range by 14%
The combination of all improvements—cell design, factory, anode, cathode, and integration—achieves Tesla's goal to halve cost per kWh
money_quotes: What a fantastic world we live in that we can witness the first Apple-like keynote for batteries.
With silicon, the cookie crumbles and gets gooey.
It is really a huge pain in the ass to have tabs from a production standpoint.

If you take a look at the 'it's a small world journey' of I am a Nickel atom and what happens to me, it's crazy, you're going around the world three times, there is a moral equivalent of digging the ditch, filling in the ditch, and digging the ditch again. It's total madness.
The level of thought and detail put into rethinking old processes from first principles to make them more efficient is inspiring.
There is a clear 'why' for every decision made that boils down to economics, not just technical gains.
posts.key_insight: Tesla's battery improvements succeed because they take a holistic, first-principles approach—optimizing cell design, manufacturing, materials, and vehicle integration together rather than focusing on a single breakthrough technology.

posts.summary: Jess Frazelle explores what it would take to build robot workshop assistants like those in Iron Man, breaking down the technical requirements for fire detection, object passing, and suit assembly. She then extends this into a broader concept: a configuration management system for manufacturing that treats physical factory layouts like code deployments. The key insight is that robotics workflows could be defined in declarative config files, enabling version-controlled 'modes' for different manufacturing processes.
key_points: Robot workshop assistants require cameras, ML models, and precise coordinate systems to perform tasks like fire detection and object passing
The 'lifesaver' robot behavior requires identifying humans, detecting reaching gestures, and calculating whether assistance is needed
Factory floor layouts could be treated as configuration files, with robots loading different code and positions based on named modes
This approach creates a 'physical build pipeline' - version-controlled manufacturing with rollback capabilities
money_quotes: What this ends up leading to is a configuration management system for manufacturing, much like a build system.
Since we are hackers we will just duct tape this to the robot arm and have a trigger on the switch to fire it programmatically.
The cool thing about this setup is now our entire factory is configured in code. We can roll back by reverting a commit or we can add more functionality by modifying the file.
It is the equivalent of a build pipeline but for manufacturing, I guess it could be considered a physical build pipeline.
I do think becoming as productive as Tony Stark could be possible, just need the funds and time to hook it all together.
posts.key_insight: Manufacturing workflows could be treated like software deployments, with declarative configuration files defining robot positions, behaviors, and artifact pipelines that can be version-controlled and switched on command.
